She can also be unkind in a precision-strike way: saying the one sentence that lands because it is true. She sometimes mistakes observation for moral seriousness. She also enjoys being the person who sees through everyone, which means she can be late to noticing when someone is actually hurt. Her guilt over Cleo is not noble and abstract. It is bound up with the fact that, on the worst night, she chose the version of events that made her own life easier. Physically, Maeve is not glamorous and knows it, but not in a self-pitying way. She’s medium height, pale in winter to the point of looking accidentally Victorian, with dark hair she usually ties badly because she never learned the sleek-girl version. She has a face that looks most expressive when she is trying not to have one. She wears her uniform as if she lost a low-stakes argument with it: blazer slightly too creased, tie never quite right, scuffed shoes she insists are “fine.” She moves with wary economy, except when angry, when she starts walking too fast and clipping doorframes with her shoulder. She doesn’t gesture much, but her eyes give away more than she thinks. Motives, allegiances, relationships: - Her immediate motive is to work out whether Cleo’s midnight text is a real request, a manipulation, or both. - Her deeper motive is uglier: she wants not to be the sort of person who left someone alone with the truth because it was inconvenient. - She is loyal in a delayed, unreliable way. Once she commits, she will do a lot. The problem is the lag. - She loves her mum but avoids being known by her. - She finds her younger brother irritating and also trusts him more than most people. - She is still emotionally aligned with Cleo in ways she would rather die than say aloud. - She has no sincere allegiance to the school as an institution, but she is still vulnerable to its social weather. Voice samples: - “The text came at 12:07, which was either deeply sinister or exactly the sort of melodramatic scheduling Cleo would have considered a mark of quality.” - “People at school said ‘drama’ the way Victorians said ‘vapours’: with the firm belief it only happened to silly girls and never involved anything actually dangerous.” - “I didn’t ignore her because I’m evil. I ignored her because it was midnight, and because once you answer Cleo after midnight, you are no longer in charge of your evening.” # Cleo Bell Cleo is Maeve’s former best friend: vivid, difficult, magnetic, and impossible to reduce to “victim.” Seventeen, same year, from a slightly grander but more brittle home life — not wealth exactly, but aspiration performed with painful commitment. Her mother is one of those women who says “girls can be so nasty” as if narrating a wildlife documentary while being nastier than most girls she’s met. Cleo learned early that presentation could save you a lot of trouble until it didn’t. Cleo and Maeve became friends because they were both clever and bored, but Cleo was always the one with scale. She made everything larger: plans, grudges, outfits, text threads, ordinary Tuesdays. She had the gift of making a corridor crossing feel like a scene change. This made her thrilling company and, in concentrated doses, exhausting. She is not a saintly wronged girl. She helped create the social language that later trapped her. She could be manipulative, self-mythologising, and casually cruel when she had an audience. But she also had a much stronger instinct than Maeve for when a joke had curdled into something else. Part of what shattered their friendship is that she expected Maeve to recognise that line in real time and was genuinely shocked when she didn’t. Specific habits and idiosyncrasies: - She sends punctuation like emotional weather. A full stop from Cleo used to mean more than most people’s declarations. - She names things no one asked to have named: benches, stairwells, periods of her life. - She has an old habit of clicking her acrylics or rings against surfaces when thinking, even after stopping the nails. - She keeps receipts, wristbands, tiny paper evidence of nights out and bad decisions in a shoebox she once called “the archive.” - She can cry beautifully when furious and finds this useful. - She knows exactly how she looks from the side and is ashamed of knowing. - She says “don’t be tragic” when she means “please don’t leave me alone with this.” - She is very good at reading weakness in groups and very bad at forgiving it in private. Her flaws are important. Cleo often performs herself, even in genuine distress. She likes control and can become punitive when she feels abandoned. She can confuse intimacy with exclusivity. She also has a tendency to make symbolic gestures instead of straightforward requests, which is part of why the blood-drop text is plausible for her. If she wants something, she often frames it in a way that will force the other person to prove they understand. This is unfair and very teenage. It also means she and Maeve are uniquely capable of hurting each other. Physically, Cleo is striking rather than classically pretty. Tallish, all angles and deliberate choices. Dark blonde hair she either styles properly or leaves in an expensive-looking mess. Sharp brows, quick eyes, a mouth that seems permanently on the verge of commentary. She wears clothes as if they are annotations. Even in school uniform she manages some tiny act of revision — a ribbon, a rolled sleeve, a pair of tights that imply mutiny. She has a social kind of stillness: she can hold herself perfectly quiet in a way that makes other people more aware of her. When upset, that control fractures into very fast movement and clipped speech. Motives, allegiances, relationships: - She wants the truth about the party-night humiliation to exist in a form that can’t be laughed away. - She does not wholly trust institutions, and she does not currently trust Maeve. - She is using Maeve partly because Maeve remembers details and because Maeve’s own implication gives her skin in the game. - She still has attachment to Maeve, but it is tangled up with fury and contempt. - She has shifting, frayed ties to former social allies who found it convenient to call her unstable. - Her main allegiance is to her own version of events surviving intact, because once that goes she fears she’ll be rewritten entirely by other people. Voice samples: - “If you’re going to betray me, at least don’t do it looking apologetic. It’s insulting.” - “No, I’m not being dramatic. I’m being specific. People always confuse the two when a girl’s involved.” - “You didn’t not know, Maeve. You decided not to know properly. That’s different, and actually worse.” # Tasha Okoye Tasha is Maeve’s uneasy ally: not a loyal best friend replacement, but someone useful, funny, and socially alive in a completely different way. Seventeen, same year, academically solid, socially adaptable, impossible to pin down because she calibrates herself slightly differently in every setting. She and Maeve were never close, but they overlap through classes, old group projects, and a shared appetite for noticing nonsense. Tasha has enough social confidence to ask the question everyone else wants to ask, but enough self-preservation not to do it for free. Background-wise, Tasha comes from a stable, busy family where competence is expected and melodrama is a luxury item. She has siblings/cousins/a whole infrastructure of people in and out of the house, which has made her practical and nosy in equal measure. She is the sort of person who has worked out where all the soft power lives in school: office staff, site staff, prefects no one notices, group chat admins, girls who know passwords. She isn’t a gossip in the empty sense; she likes information because information is useful. She is funny in a much more external way than Maeve. Maeve’s humour is defensive and surgical; Tasha’s is social and kinetic. She can make someone laugh while also extracting what she wants from them. She is not above enjoying chaos, but she has much better survival instincts than the narrator. The reason she helps is not pure goodness. She wants to know what happened, she dislikes the current version of events, and she enjoys having a role in something bigger than coursework and UCAS. Specific habits and idiosyncrasies: - She borrows pens and actually returns them, which makes people trust her. - She has a private taxonomy for teachers based on footwear. - She is always eating something from a crumpled packet and remains annoyingly elegant about it. - She can find almost anyone in school within ten minutes. - She says “interesting” in a way that can mean “promising,” “pathetic,” or “absolutely not.” - She keeps her phone brightness bizarrely low. - She has no patience for cryptic nonsense if a direct question would do, but enjoys mocking the cryptic nonsense while she solves it. Her flaws: she can be opportunistic, and there are moments where she helps because she likes being in possession of the sharpest version of the story. She also tends to underestimate emotional damage if it doesn’t show itself efficiently. She is not cruel, but she can be brisk to the point of coldness. She doesn’t always grasp the depth of Maeve and Cleo’s bond because from the outside it looked unbearable. Physically, Tasha has brisk, grounded energy. Slightly shorter than Maeve, athletic without making a thing of it, hair usually immaculate in a way that suggests actual discipline rather than luck. She occupies space confidently and leans in when interested, which can feel like warmth or interrogation. Her face is highly expressive when she chooses to let it be. She can switch from amused to appalled in half a second. She moves through school as if there are fewer barriers for her than for other people, which is partly confidence and partly because she talks to everyone as if she’s already allowed there. Motives, allegiances, relationships: - She wants the truth largely because she hates manipulated narratives and because someone is clearly gaming the situation. - She is on Maeve’s side to the extent that Maeve is currently the most useful route to the truth. - She is not anti-Cleo, but she finds Cleo’s style of communication infuriating. - She is socially adjacent to almost everyone and therefore trusted by no one entirely. - Her allegiance is to clarity, with a side order of entertainment. Voice samples: - “Right, well, that’s either a cry for help or the most embarrassing attempt at menace I’ve ever seen.” - “I’m not saying she’s lying. I’m saying if she wanted to be believed, perhaps she could occasionally speak like a person and not an A24 trailer.” - “You two are exhausting. I mean this with affection, but if Gothic yearning was an extracurricular you’d both have colours.” # Ryan Lacey Ryan is the student most responsible for the original harm and the current cover-up. He should not feel like a sleek villain imported from another book. He is seventeen, socially well-positioned, superficially easygoing, from the kind of family that is locally respected in a bland and sturdy way. He has grown up being read as decent. Not dazzling, not terrifying — safe. This is part of what protects him. Ryan is good at the low-level management of group dynamics. He knows how to make something sound less serious than it is and how to make anyone objecting seem intense. On the party night, he did not set out to destroy someone’s life in grand terms. He set out to win a moment, amuse people, keep status on his side, and avoid looking weak. Then, when it became obvious that the “joke” had gone too far, he moved instantly into self-protection. That is what makes him dangerous: not cartoon malice, but a strong instinct to preserve his own version of himself at all costs. He is not emotionally profound, but he is socially intelligent. He knows exactly when to say “I genuinely feel awful if anyone took it like that” in the tone of someone granting a concession. He is also very capable of believing his own edited story. Months later, he may sincerely think the real wrong is everyone making such a massive issue of one bad night. Specific habits and idiosyncrasies: - Says people’s names slightly too often in conversation, like he’s already halfway through conflict resolution. - Keeps his voice level even when cornered, which reads as maturity until it doesn’t. - Has one over-practised gesture — rubbing the back of his neck, palms out, little laugh — that he deploys whenever pretending things have got weird for reasons beyond his control. - He is good at talking to parents and staff because he has spent years being harmless in front of them. - He was one of those boys who got away with saying sharper things because he said them almost kindly. - He dislikes uncontrolled emotion in others because it destabilises his preferred version of reality. His flaws are obvious once seen: cowardice, vanity, and a real talent for moral downgrading. He can recategorise cruelty as misunderstanding in seconds. He also underestimates girls in a very ordinary way; he assumes they will either implode or get over it. What he never fully accounts for is persistence. Physically, Ryan is the sort of boy teachers describe as “solid.” Taller than most, broad-shouldered, tidy without trying too hard, the kind of face that photographs as more sincere than it really is. Hair always acceptable. Blazer worn properly. He moves with the confidence of someone for whom corridors have rarely been hostile. He stands close enough to imply ease, not threat. His expression tends towards patient concern, which in the right light becomes deeply aggravating. When actually rattled, his jaw tightens before anything else does. Motives, allegiances, relationships: - His motive is straightforward: stop the old incident from becoming newly legible. - He is loyal primarily to himself, secondarily to the friend-group version of events that protects him. - He likely thinks of Maeve as clever but erratic, and of Cleo as someone who made everything worse by refusing to let it die. - He has allies not because they love him but because accepting the truth would implicate them too. - He does not think of himself as a bad person. That is central. Voice samples: - “Come on, Maeve. You know that’s not what happened.” - “It was stupid, yeah. Obviously. But you’re acting like it was some kind of coordinated attack.” - “I’ve apologised for my part. I can’t really do much about people deciding to turn it into folklore.” # Mrs Viv Bell Cleo’s mother, not a main viewpoint character but important to the atmosphere around Cleo. Viv is one of those intensely managed women who seem permanently five minutes from hosting something no one asked for. She is polished, brittle, and very invested in surfaces, partly because she has had to be. Her relationship with Cleo is a mix of genuine concern, competition, exasperation, and projection. She is not a villain, but she is exactly the sort of parent who can make a daughter feel lonelier while technically being attentive. She would have been socially formidable at school and still behaves as if every interaction may be reputationally consequential. She dislikes untidiness in all forms, including emotional untidiness. If Cleo’s suffering cannot be narrated in a respectable sequence, Viv loses patience with it. This helps explain why Cleo has become someone who prefers coded gestures and dramatic indirection to plain need. Physicality: immaculate blow-dry, expensive trainers with “I’m practical actually” energy, smile that can become a line in under a second. She moves quickly and with purpose, always carrying too much. She touches people briefly when performing warmth. Motives, allegiances, relationships: - Wants Cleo safe, but possibly even more wants the situation containable. - Sees Maeve as both part of the problem and one of the few people who actually mattered to Cleo. - Would be excellent in a small emergency and poor in a long emotional truth. Voice samples: - “I’m not saying the school handled it brilliantly, darling, but feeding it further isn’t always wise.” - “Cleo has a gift for intensity. You mustn’t mistake that for reliability.” - “We are not doing this by rumour, thank you.” # Jonah Hargreaves Maeve’s younger brother, fourteen, useful precisely because he is not part of the sixth-form social theatre. Jonah is a needling, observant younger sibling with the irritating moral clarity of someone who still thinks hypocrisy should be easy to avoid. He lives half in his own online worlds and half in the practical reality of the house: who’s home, who’s lying, what’s gone missing, whether Mum’s in a mood. Jonah and Maeve communicate mostly through insult. He notices when she is spiralling before she does and has no tact about it. Because he is younger, people talk around him, which means he often picks up useful things. He is also exactly the kind of person to find a clue by accident because he has borrowed the wrong charger, opened the wrong browser tab, or rifled through a pile looking for batteries. Specific habits and idiosyncrasies: - Eats dry cereal directly from the box and denies this is antisocial. - Says “grim” with devastating precision. - Remembers passwords he has no business remembering. - Has no respect for sixth formers as a species. - Is deeply annoying about obvious truths. Physicality: all elbows, growing too fast, fringe in his eyes, school jumper permanently skewed. He moves like someone in a hurry to become taller. Speaks from doorways. Motives, allegiances, relationships: - Loyal to Maeve in the unquestioning sibling way that disguises itself as contempt. - Thinks Cleo is “a nightmare” but also understands she mattered. - No allegiance to school drama, which makes him surprisingly useful as an outside angle. Voice samples: - “If you’re trying to act normal, maybe don’t stare at your phone like it’s got a bomb in it.” - “I’m not being rude, I’m being correct. There’s a difference.” - “You always say someone’s dramatic right before it turns out they were right, so maybe retire that one.” # Mr Simon Firth A pastoral lead / sixth-form tutor figure. Mid-thirties or forties, not malicious, not a caricature, but institutionally house-trained. He is one of the adults who nearly helps. He believes in process, safeguarding forms, calm voices, and not making things worse — which in practice often means helping situations become administratively legible only after the emotional damage is done. He likely likes Maeve in the abstract way staff like intelligent difficult students: she is one of the few who listens with actual contempt, which at least is engagement. He may also have been one of the adults involved in the original fallout, meaning he has his own need to think it was handled reasonably. Physicality: tired suit, lanyard, apologetic posture, perpetually carrying a mug of tea he never finishes. He sits forward when trying to seem available. Rubs his temple when teenagers are being more complex than his training slides. Motives, allegiances, relationships: - Wants students safe, but through procedure. - Wants no public scandal if possible. - Is more trustworthy than most adults in the story, which is not saying enough. - May become important because he half-believes Maeve before he fully can. Voice samples: - “I am taking this seriously, Maeve. I need you to understand that taking something seriously and reacting immediately are not always the same thing.” - “From where I’m sitting, there are several overlapping issues here.” - “I appreciate that for you this feels urgent. I’m not dismissing that.”

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸 At 12:07, to be exact, which I know because I looked at it for a solid fifteen seconds without breathing properly, like time might change if I stared hard enough. My room was dark except for the phone screen and the orange smear of the streetlamp coming through the gap in the curtain. There was rain needling at the window in that annoying, committed way it has here, like weather with a grievance. Jonah had gone to bed an hour earlier after yelling at someone through his headset about camping, which I gather is a military term now and not just what failed middle-class dads do in August. Mum was on late shift and not due back till half one. So there was no one awake to witness me behaving exactly like the sort of girl I spent a lot of time insisting I wasn’t. The message was from Cleo. Not “Cleo” because I’d deleted her name in some bleak fit of principle in September. Just the number. But I knew it anyway. You don’t spend three years textually entangled with someone without learning the shape of their existence by heart. There are people I couldn’t pick out of a line-up if they were on fire, but I’d know Cleo Bell’s number in a coma. 🩸🩸🩸 No words. No “u up?” No “please answer.” No explanation. Just three blood drops sitting there in the thread like they owned the place. My first thought, embarrassingly, was not fear. It was: for God’s sake. That probably makes me sound evil. In my defence, Cleo and I had once used the blood-drop emoji to mean all sorts of things, most of them stupid. It started with periods, obviously. Not in a heartfelt feminist solidarity way. In the practical way fifteen-year-old girls do things, which is to text from a cubicle with all the urgency of a hostage negotiator because Year 10 taught us two important truths: biology waits for no one, and school toilet paper is decorative. Then it became code. One blood drop meant Bring pads. Two meant Emergency but mainly embarrassing. Three meant Come outside now or I’ll die / kill someone / set fire to my own life. Which was a joke. Until, in the Cleo way, it stopped being entirely one. There were other meanings too. Over time she used the same three drops for You are dead to me, and Did you see what she posted, and If you don’t answer I’m climbing in your window. With Cleo, symbols had a habit of multiplying like mould. She never saw why a thing should mean one thing if it could mean six and all of them make the recipient sweat. I should have ignored it. That sounds nobler than the truth, which is that I nearly replied immediately, but only to say what the hell now. My thumb actually hovered over the keyboard before pride kicked in. We hadn’t had a real conversation in months. We’d had one exchange in October about coursework where she wrote “have u done the bibliography” as if we were two women discussing rail replacement services. Then nothing. Nothing direct, anyway. Which is the modern teenage equivalent of saying we’d fought a war. I locked the phone, unlocked it again, then read the message a second time as if blood drops might reveal hidden text on a reread. No typing bubble appeared. No follow-up. No dramatic “Maeve?” as punctuation. That was what got me, I think. Cleo without commentary was like finding a swan in Aldi. Something had gone wrong at the level of nature. I put the phone face-down on the duvet and lay back, staring at the ceiling. The sensible explanation was obvious. She was bored, drunk, spiralling, trying to drag me into one of her late-night symbolic episodes because everyone else had got wise or blocked her or gone to sleep. She had form for all three. And yet. There are some messages that arrive carrying the full weight of who sent them. If anyone else had texted me three blood drops after midnight, I’d have assumed gang initiation, period trouble or a deeply poor sense of humour. From Cleo, it came with a whole undertow of old meanings, some of them mine. I checked her profile picture. Nothing. No weird update, no black screen, no dramatic lyric. Last seen hidden, because of course it was. I opened our old messages, which is a grotesque thing to do after midnight if you’d like to preserve any remaining fragments of self-respect. Whole eras of us sat there in the dark. Look alive x Can u see Ryan by the bikes I’m actually going to kill myself if she wears that hat tomorrow 🩸🩸🩸 bring supplies What supplies ALL SUPPLIES That means literally nothing It should have been funny. Some of it was. We had been funny, which was part of the issue. I told myself, quite firmly, that I was not doing this. I was not getting hauled back into Cleo Bell’s haunted little weather system because she’d sent me three wet cartoon organs in the night. I had school in the morning. I had a politics essay in disgraceful condition. I had, theoretically, matured. So I put my phone on Do Not Disturb, turned over, and spent forty-five minutes not sleeping. At 12:58 the front door went downstairs. Keys, a muffled swear, Mum dropping something in the kitchen. I nearly went down. Not because I wanted to tell her about the message — absolutely not, I’m not deranged — but because the idea of another human being in the house made my room feel less like the sort of place where old mistakes texted you after midnight. I didn’t go. Instead I listened to cupboard doors and kettle sounds and Mum humming one line of something tuneless to herself, then eventually I slept in that thin, breakable way where your dreams know your phone is nearby. In the morning, the message was still there. That was somehow offensive. I got ready in a foul mood. Grey sky, wet pavement, hair refusing any known arrangement. Jonah was in the kitchen eating dry cereal straight from the box and watching some video of a man restoring a knife for reasons too depressing to ask about. “You look rancid,” he said. “Morning to you too.” “No, like medically. Did you sleep?” “Obviously.” He gave me the sort of look only a younger brother can give, one which says I know you’re lying and also your entire generation is emotionally unfit for service. Mum was already gone again, having left toast under foil and a note saying Pharmacy audit. Back by six. x. Her handwriting always looked apologetic even when she was writing things like buy bin bags. I checked my phone while the kettle boiled. Still nothing from Cleo. No second message. No explanation. Just the three blood drops and, above them, the time: 12:07. And then something cold and neat slid into place in my stomach. 12:07. There are timestamps your brain keeps against your will. I had not thought about that one in weeks, maybe months, in the disciplined way people say they’re not thinking about a thing while arranging their entire lives around not touching it. But there it was all the same. 12:07 was the timestamp on the video. Not the whole video. The bit at the top of the screenshot Tasha had shown me in September before I told her to delete it and she said, “Bit late for moral hygiene, babes.” Grainy corridor light. Someone laughing off-camera. Cleo turning towards the lens, already knowing. 12:07. Jonah crunched cereal behind me. The kettle clicked off. I opened the message again and for the first time it didn’t feel dramatic or pathetic or annoying. It felt deliberate. Then my phone buzzed in my hand, and I nearly threw it into the sink.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a genuinely impressive opening chapter that demonstrates strong command of voice, tone, and the specific demands of darkly comic British YA fiction. Let me work through the key elements critically. VOICE AND CHARACTER: The narrator's voice is the chapter's greatest strength. Maeve's defensive intelligence comes through consistently and without strain. Lines like "I'm not deranged" and "I had, theoretically, matured" land with exactly the right self-aware irony. The categorisation of the blood-drop emoji's meanings (one drop, two drops, three drops) is both funny and efficient world-building. The voice never tips into trying too hard — it maintains the dry, slightly exhausted register of someone who has made a habit of underreacting. The Jonah scene is particularly well-handled: "You look rancid" is exactly right for a fourteen-year-old brother, and his cereal-eating detail is used without being belaboured. METAPHORS AND POETIC RIFFING: Most of the figurative language earns its place. "Like finding a swan in Aldi" is genuinely funny and culturally specific. "Rain with a grievance" works. "Symbols had a habit of multiplying like mould" is apt. "Thin, breakable way where your dreams know your phone is nearby" is perhaps the most ambitious image and it mostly lands — it captures a specific kind of anxious half-sleep. There are a couple of moments where the prose reaches slightly: "the full weight of who sent them" is a touch generic, and "cold and neat slid into place in my stomach" is a familiar construction. But these are minor. The chapter does not suffer from the purple prose or forced metaphor problem that plagues much YA writing. PLOT AND STRUCTURE: The chapter follows its plan faithfully. We get the midnight text, the history of the emoji code, Maeve's defensive non-response, the morning-after scene with Jonah, and the crucial revelation of the 12:07 timestamp. The structure is clean without feeling mechanical. The cliffhanger — the phone buzzing in her hand — is appropriately calibrated: not overwrought, just enough. SHOW VS TELL: The chapter is largely showing rather than telling, which is impressive given how much backstory needs establishing. The history of Maeve and Cleo's friendship is conveyed through the old message thread excerpt rather than exposition. The mum is established through a note and kettle sounds rather than description. However, there is one passage — "There are some messages that arrive carrying the full weight of who sent them" — that slides into telling-mode and is the weakest sentence in the chapter. It's the kind of observation that sounds profound but is actually doing the work of explaining something the reader should be allowed to feel. DIALOGUE: There is very little dialogue, but what exists is strong. Jonah's lines are pitch-perfect. The old message thread excerpt is cleverly deployed as a form of dialogue that also characterises both girls efficiently. COHERENCE AND CONSISTENCY WITH PROFILES: Maeve is exactly as described in the character profile — defensive, funny, stubborn, prone to categorisation, bad at sleeping, good at timestamps. The detail about her knowing Cleo's number "in a coma" is consistent with her compulsive memory for details. Cleo is present only through her absence and the text, but the characterisation of her emoji use as meaning-multiplying is consistent with the profile's description of her as someone who makes symbols do too much work. CHAPTER PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter hits every beat from the plan: the midnight text, the history of the code, the near-reply, the morning decision that it was attention-seeking, and the crucial 12:07 revelation. The plan called for ending on her realising the text was "aimed very precisely at her memory" — the chapter delivers this with the timestamp revelation and the video reference, which is handled with appropriate restraint (we get enough to understand something bad happened without over-explaining). DARK COMEDY: The balance between comedy and dread is well-managed. The chapter is genuinely funny in places without undercutting the growing unease. The Jonah scene provides comic relief that feels earned rather than inserted. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is perhaps slightly too comfortable in its own voice — there are moments where the wit feels slightly performed, where you can sense the author enjoying the prose a little too much. The reference to the video and Tasha ("before I told her to delete it and she said, 'Bit late for moral hygiene, babes'") introduces a character and a piece of information that feels slightly rushed — it's doing a lot of work in a single parenthetical. The cliffhanger is effective but conventional. Overall, this is a very strong opening chapter that demonstrates genuine craft, a distinctive voice, and faithful execution of a well-conceived plan. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The buzz was only my phone reminding me I had not done my politics reading, which felt petty even for technology. I stared at the notification until it dissolved back into the lock screen. Jonah watched me over his cereal with open contempt. “If you’re trying to act normal,” he said, “maybe don’t look like that.” “Like what?” “Like your phone’s just confessed.” I told him to shut up, which in our house is effectively tenderness, and left five minutes later in a blazer that still smelled faintly of damp from last week. The rain had given up by then, but not with grace. Everything outside looked rubbed grey. The bus was late, naturally, and when it did come it was full of Year 9s behaving like a public inquiry had concluded adults no longer had authority. Ordinarily I would have put my headphones in and ignored the world. Instead I sat there with Cleo’s message open in one hand and the bus fogging up around me, trying not to think in circles. There were a dozen explanations and all of them were bad in specific ways. By the time I got to school, I’d half-convinced myself the timestamp was coincidence. Human beings are excellent at this. We call it perspective when what we mean is selective cowardice. School looked exactly as it always did after rain: the main building damp and mildly disappointed in us, the windows streaked, the fake-cheerful Sixth Form banner outside reception listing values no one had ever once displayed near the vending machines. Resilience. Community. Curiosity. There should have been one saying plausible deniability and left it at that. I cut through the lower corridor toward form and nearly got taken out by two Year 11 boys fake-fighting with umbrellas. “Language, please,” called Mr Firth from nowhere, with the odd priestly timing of pastoral staff. Neither of them had actually said anything, but they nodded anyway because adults in school often seem to operate on frequencies inaccessible to the rest of us. “Maeve,” he added, spotting me. “You all right?” This is one of the reasons pastoral teachers are exhausting. They ask “you all right?” in a tone that makes any honest answer feel both melodramatic and administratively unhelpful. “Thriving,” I said. He gave me the look of a man deciding whether sarcasm counts as disclosure, then let it go. “Don’t forget personal statements by Friday.” “Cruel.” That got half a smile, which was probably what he wanted. Evidence of life. Tick. Form was form: damp sleeves, low-level panic, everyone pretending UCAS was not a bloodsport. Tasha slid into the chair behind me three minutes late with a croissant in a paper bag and the expression of someone prepared to be reasonable only under protest. “You’ve got that face,” she said quietly. “What face?” “The one where you’ve either committed a crime or remembered one.” “Bit early.” “Interesting non-denial.” I ignored her and opened my planner to a page I had no intention of reading. Tasha let it sit for all of ten seconds, which for her is spiritual restraint. “Was it Cleo?” I turned round. She shrugged, tearing off a piece of croissant. “You’ve only looked haunted by one person for this long.” This is what I mean about Tasha. She says things in a tone suggesting she’s discussing weather patterns while actually inserting a knife between your ribs. “I got a text,” I said. “From?” I looked at her. “Oh my God.” She sat back. “That is so much better than coursework.” “Your moral centre is a puddle.” “It’s not immoral to prefer a live crisis to constitutional reform.” I showed her the message under the desk. She read it, then raised her eyebrows. “Right,” she said. “That’s either a cry for help or the most embarrassing attempt at menace I’ve ever seen.” “It was sent at 12:07.” That changed her face. Not massively. Just enough. “Ah,” she said. “So not random, then.” No, not random. No one ever called it anything official, the blood-drop thing. That would have been too organised, too self-aware. It wasn’t one of those sinister school traditions in American films where everyone wears masks and swears oaths in Latin. It was just something that spread. First it was Cleo and me, because of course it was. We were fifteen and bored and very pleased with ourselves. If one of us wanted the other out of a lesson, or in the toilets, or at the back gate with supplies or gossip or a fake emergency, we sent blood. Stupid and dramatic and ours. Then other people started nicking it, because that is what happens to anything with enough style and not enough dignity. By Year 11, girls were sending one another blood drops before dares, humiliations, prank confessions, “you’ve been chosen” nonsense. If you got it, there’d usually be something waiting: your bag hidden, your crush texted from your phone, your name Sharpied onto a sanitary bin in the loos if someone was feeling particularly enlightened. It was not a cult. It was not folklore. It was the kind of low-grade cruelty school specialises in because no one wants to be the person who says actually this is weird. Half the time it was funny. The other half it was only funny if it wasn’t happening to you. And, because everyone involved was a coward in some arrangement, it always travelled under softer names. Banter. A joke. Drama. By break, I’d heard “drama” twice and “Cleo being Cleo” once, and neither was even about my text. Her name still moved around school like mould in a ceiling corner: unsightly, persistent, easier to ignore if you didn’t look directly at it. At the lockers, two girls from Year 12 were talking in that fake-casual whisper people use when they absolutely want to be overheard. “I just think if she wanted people on her side,” one of them said, “she could have maybe not—” She stopped when she saw me. Not because she cared. Because gossip has standards, apparently. I smiled at her before I could help it, which made her flinch and me hate myself. Then I yanked my locker open harder than necessary. Something small and red dropped out and hit the floor. Not blood. Not even close. Three jelly sweets in the shape of little hearts, the cheap shiny sort dusted in sugar, rolled in a neat line toward my shoe. For one stupid second I just stared. Around me, corridor noise kept going: locker doors, shouting, someone laughing too hard at something mediocre. No music swell, no dramatic hush. Real life is rude like that. Taped to the inside of my locker door, half hidden by my timetable, was a scrap of lined paper. It had only one thing on it. 🩸🩸🩸 Not the actual emoji, obviously. Someone had drawn them in red pen. Badly. Behind me, Tasha said, “Well. That’s not ideal.” I peeled the note off and folded it into my fist. “This is stupid,” I said. “Mm.” “It’s literally jelly hearts. That is aggressively naff.” “Also true.” “This is someone being twelve.” Tasha looked at me carefully. “You keep saying that like it’s making you calmer.” I shut the locker and there, reflected faintly in the metal for half a second before the corridor swallowed him, was Ryan Lacey at the far end of the hall, talking to someone from rugby with his easy, patient little smile. Then he glanced up. Not at the sweets. Not at the note. Straight at me.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong chapter that demonstrates genuine command of voice, social texture, and tonal control. Let me assess it carefully across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Maeve's first-person narration is consistently excellent. The defensive intelligence described in her profile comes through in nearly every paragraph. Lines like "We call it perspective when what we mean is selective cowardice" and the observation about pastoral teachers asking "you all right?" in a tone that makes honest answers feel "melodramatic and administratively unhelpful" are genuinely sharp and feel earned rather than performed. The voice is specific, funny, and self-aware without being smug. Tasha's characterisation is also well-handled — her dialogue lands with the right mix of social ease and mild opportunism. "Your moral centre is a puddle" / "It's not immoral to prefer a live crisis to constitutional reform" is a good exchange that feels natural rather than scripted. Jonah's brief appearance at the opening is pitch-perfect: the "like your phone's just confessed" line is exactly the kind of needling younger-sibling observation his profile calls for. PLOT AND STRUCTURE: The chapter follows its plan closely. We get the school social ecosystem, the blood-drop tradition explained without over-formalising it, the physical escalation from digital text to locker note and jelly sweets, and the chapter closes on Ryan Lacey's glance — a clean, effective beat that moves the plot forward without melodrama. The plan called for the protagonist to "ask a few careful questions and get nowhere useful" before finding the physical provocation, and this is delivered. The chapter doesn't over-explain or rush. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is one of the chapter's genuine strengths. It's clipped, character-specific, and avoids the over-polished quality the plan warned against. "That's either a cry for help or the most embarrassing attempt at menace I've ever seen" is a Tasha line that feels completely in character. Mr Firth's brief appearance is handled well — his "you all right?" and the sarcasm exchange are economical and true. SOCIAL REALISM: The British school setting is rendered with real specificity. The Sixth Form banner listing values ("Resilience. Community. Curiosity."), the Year 9s on the bus, the Year 11 umbrella fight, the pastoral teacher's odd timing — these details accumulate into a convincing institutional texture without becoming a list. The explanation of the blood-drop tradition is particularly well done: "It was not a cult. It was not folklore. It was the kind of low-grade cruelty school specialises in because no one wants to be the person who says actually this is weird." This is exactly the right register. METAPHOR AND PROSE QUALITY: Most of the figurative language lands. "Cleo's name still moved around school like mould in a ceiling corner: unsightly, persistent, easier to ignore if you didn't look directly at it" is a good image — specific and slightly ugly in the right way. The school building being "mildly disappointed in us" is a nice touch. There are a couple of moments where the prose reaches slightly — "the rain had given up by then, but not with grace" is fine but not exceptional — but nothing tips into purple prose or forced poetry. The writing is disciplined. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is very strong but not quite flawless. The ending with Ryan's glance is effective but slightly telegraphed — the reader has been primed to suspect him, and the chapter doesn't quite subvert that expectation. The jelly sweets as physical escalation are a good idea but the "aggressively naff" quality Maeve notes is perhaps slightly too cute — the chapter acknowledges the bathos but doesn't fully exploit it. The two Year 12 girls at the lockers are a functional gossip-delivery mechanism but feel slightly thin compared to the other social textures. The chapter also doesn't quite deliver on the plan's note about the protagonist asking "careful questions" — she mostly receives information rather than actively seeking it, which is a minor deviation. OVERALL: This is confident, well-crafted YA fiction with a genuinely distinctive voice. It follows the chapter plan faithfully, maintains character consistency, and achieves the tonal balance of dark comedy and social realism the prompt calls for. The lightness earns its keep — the humour doesn't deflect from the underlying tension but coexists with it. A very good chapter with minor imperfections. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Ryan didn’t do anything dramatic. That would have been useful. He just looked at me for half a second too long, like he was checking whether I’d seen him seeing me, and then went back to whatever bland male alliance was happening by the science stairs. It was such a normal movement that I instantly hated myself for noticing it. “Don’t,” said Tasha. “I didn’t say anything.” “You did a whole internal documentary with your face.” “It was one expression.” “It was five, layered.” I shoved the jelly hearts into my blazer pocket because leaving them on the floor felt deranged and throwing them away felt somehow weak. Then we went to lesson. I retained nothing from double politics except that Mr Jansen has somehow made coalition theory sound like a minor digestive complaint and that Tasha wrote on the corner of my worksheet: if this turns out to be year 13 flirting I will kill you myself. I wrote back: with jelly sweets? She looked at it, nearly smiled, and underlined flirting twice as if to show she had standards. By lunch I had reached that point where dread starts making you practical. Not calm, exactly. Efficient in odd little bursts. “Tasha,” I said, catching up with her outside the canteen, “show me the screenshot.” She stopped. “You told me to delete it.” “I know.” “And I did.” “You absolutely did not.” “Correct,” she said. “I’m not an idiot.” We took our lunch — chips, because if your life is collapsing there should at least be chips — to the Sixth Form study room, which at lunchtime was mostly empty because everyone prefers to suffer publicly. Rain was starting again outside, silvering the windows. Tasha sat opposite me, dug through her camera roll with the concentration of a surgeon, and turned the phone around. There it was. A screenshot of a video no one was meant to have anymore. Grainy corridor lighting, the horrible yellow sort the school uses after hours when it wants to imply a duty of care but not spend money. The timestamp at the top: 12:07. Cleo half turned towards the camera. Mouth open as if speaking. One hand lifted. Someone just out of frame on the left. It was nothing, really. If you didn’t know. That was the genius of it. “Where did you get this?” I asked. “Ria sent it to me in September because she thought I’d enjoy the social ecology.” “You’re a ghoul.” “Thank you. She deleted it after. I kept it because one day, I thought, this may become useful or ruinous.” I looked at Cleo’s face again. Grainy, blurred, but recognisably her. She looked angry. Or frightened. Or both. The thing about screenshots is they trap a moment without helping you at all. “Can you get the actual video?” “Probably not. If I could, I would have sold it for influence by now.” I ignored that. “Who else got sent the blood drops?” Tasha reached for a chip. “Possibly no one. Possibly everyone. Depends how committed your little ghost is.” “That’s not helping.” “No, but this might. I heard Freya Lomax got something in her bag this morning.” “Freya?” That surprised me enough to be useful. Freya was in Year 12 and had the skittish, expensive energy of someone whose parents describe holidays as investments. She and Cleo had never had anything to do with each other. “Three red lollies tied with dental floss. Very serial-killer craft fair. She binned them because she’s allergic to atmosphere.” “Why her?” Tasha tipped one shoulder. “Maybe it means nothing. Maybe it means she saw something. Maybe your sender’s just gone broad.” None of that helped. Which is to say, all of it did. We spent the next twenty minutes doing what passes for investigation when you are seventeen and under-supervised: trawling old message threads, checking tagged photos from the summer, following who had suddenly unfollowed whom and when. It was ridiculous, except that this is actually where half of modern truth lives — not in confessions, but in petty digital weather. Cleo’s Instagram was still private. Profile unchanged. One photo archived, though, because there was a gap where I knew a post used to be: her on someone’s shoulders at the party in a silver top, looking expensive and doomed. Ryan’s account was public in the smug male way, all rugby mud and pub lighting and one surprisingly tender picture with his dog that probably did more for him socially than all his GCSEs combined. “What’s this?” Tasha said, tapping the screen. It was an old close-friends story highlight on somebody else’s account, one I would never have looked at because the girl in question once called me “intense” in Year 11 and I’ve never fully recovered. A blurry clip from the same night. Music too loud, camera swinging. People in someone’s kitchen yelling lyrics no one knew. At the edge of the frame, barely there, Ryan leaning in close to someone by the back door. Not Cleo. Freya. “That’s odd,” I said. “Mm.” “Could be nothing.” “Everything can be nothing if you’re committed enough,” Tasha said. “That’s practically the school motto.” I zoomed. Useless. Pixels became theology. “What’s the event this week?” I asked. “Sixth Form open evening on Thursday. Why?” Because suddenly I remembered a conversation half-overheard yesterday in the corridor, Ryan saying to someone, “No, Thursday’s better, everyone’ll be here.” At the time I’d assumed rugby or some jointly managed male disaster. Now it came back with too much shape. “Nothing,” I said. “Maybe nothing.” Tasha looked at me in a way that suggested she was once again tolerating my personality under severe strain. “I hate mysteries run by people with A-levels,” she said. “At least proper criminals commit.” After lunch I did something I absolutely hadn’t planned to do and went looking for Freya. I found her outside the English block standing under the overhang with two friends and a coffee the colour of varnish. She looked at me the way girls like Freya look at girls like me, which is to say as if I were either about to ask for help with a printer or mention Marx. “Did you get something in your bag this morning?” I said, because subtlety had, by then, been exhausted. Her friends immediately became architecture. Freya blinked. “What?” “Three red sweets. Or something like that.” There was a tiny pause. Very small. But enough. “No?” “You sure?” She gave a little laugh, badly aimed. “Why would I not be sure what’s in my own bag?” This was going so well. “Because maybe you threw it out without thinking.” “Maeve,” she said, lowering her voice with the solemnity of someone discussing local tax policy, “if this is about Cleo, I’m honestly not interested.” There it was. Not denial. Deflection. “I didn’t say it was.” “You didn’t have to.” I might still have pushed if one of her friends hadn’t said, with awful brightness, “God, is Cleo back?” in exactly the tone people use for mildew. Freya shot her a look. Too quick, too frightened. Then she said, “I’ve got class,” and left with both of them, coffee in hand, spine very straight. Tasha, who had appeared at some point during this exchange like a witness summoned for sport, exhaled through her nose. “Well,” she said. “That was subtle and terrible.” “She was lying.” “Probably.” “And she’s scared.” “Yes.” I looked at the rain darkening the paving stones. My phone felt heavier in my pocket than a phone should. “Thursday,” I said. Tasha nodded once. “Thursday.” At the end of the day, as we were leaving, she caught my sleeve. “One more thing.” She showed me her phone. A screenshot of Cleo’s number in her contacts. Under it, one unread message received at 1:14 a.m. Not words. Just 🩸🩸🩸.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of YA fiction that largely delivers on its plan and maintains the voice established in the character profiles. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically. VOICE AND CHARACTER Maeve's voice is consistently rendered. The defensive intelligence, the compulsive noticing, the self-lacerating humour — all present and functioning. Lines like "Mr Jansen has somehow made coalition theory sound like a minor digestive complaint" are exactly the right register: dry, specific, not trying too hard. The exchange with Tasha about the worksheet ("with jelly sweets?" / underlining "flirting twice as if to show she had standards") is genuinely funny and characterful without being overwritten. Tasha is well-deployed. Her line "I hate mysteries run by people with A-levels" is one of the chapter's best — it's funny, it's in character, and it does social work. Her function as the practical, slightly opportunistic ally who keeps the investigation moving without becoming a plot device is handled well. She borrows pens and returns them; she kept the screenshot because "one day this may become useful or ruinous" — these are consistent with her profile. Ryan's brief appearance is handled with real skill. "He just looked at me for half a second too long, like he was checking whether I'd seen him seeing me" — this is exactly the right kind of menace for this story: not theatrical, not cartoonish, just the ordinary social intelligence of someone managing a situation. The chapter plan called for him to be believable rather than villainous, and this delivers. Freya's scene is the chapter's most interesting new element. The deflection rather than denial ("I didn't say it was." / "You didn't have to.") is sharp writing. Her friends becoming "architecture" is a nice touch. The fear in her quick look is shown rather than told. PLOT AND STRUCTURE The chapter follows its plan closely: the investigation is messy and teenage, the digital archaeology feels authentic (unfollows, archived posts, close-friends story highlights), and the chapter ends with the clue pointing toward Thursday's open evening. The revelation that Tasha also received the blood-drop message is a good closing beat — it expands the mystery without resolving it, and the 1:14 a.m. timestamp adds a layer of precision that echoes the 12:07 detail from Chapter 1. The discovery of Freya's connection to Ryan via the old story highlight is handled with appropriate ambiguity — "Could be nothing" / "Everything can be nothing if you're committed enough" — which is both funny and thematically apt for a story about how truth gets managed. WEAKNESSES The chapter is not without problems. "Pixels became theology" is the kind of line that sounds clever but doesn't quite land — it's reaching for profundity in a way that slightly jars with the otherwise grounded voice. It's a minor instance of the forced-poetry tendency the plan explicitly warned against. "The thing about screenshots is they trap a moment without helping you at all" is a little too neat, a touch of tell-don't-show dressed up as observation. It's the kind of line that would work better if it were more specific to Maeve's situation rather than a general aphorism. The description of Ryan's Instagram as "smug male way, all rugby mud and pub lighting and one surprisingly tender picture with his dog that probably did more for him socially than all his GCSEs combined" is good, but the GCSE joke is slightly over-extended. The chapter is perhaps slightly too smooth in its pacing — the investigation moves efficiently, but the plan called for it to be "messy, funny, and teenage," and while it is funny and teenage, it's perhaps a little too competent. The "wrong guesses" element from the plan is somewhat absent; the investigation mostly points in the right direction. The chips detail ("because if your life is collapsing there should at least be chips") is a nice grounding touch, but it's also the kind of thing that can feel like performed British-YA-ness if not careful. Here it just about earns its place. DIALOGUE The dialogue is generally strong. It doesn't sound polished in the wrong way — it sounds like people who are smart and slightly performing their smartness for each other, which is exactly right for these characters. The Freya exchange is the best dialogue in the chapter: terse, loaded, socially specific. TONE The dark comedy is well-calibrated. The chapter doesn't tip into either pure comedy or pure dread; it holds both, which is the hardest thing to do. The rain silvering the windows is the one moment where the prose reaches for atmosphere in a slightly generic way, but it's brief. FAITHFULNESS TO PLAN The chapter hits its key beats: the investigation, the digital archaeology, the discovery that someone else received the message, the clue pointing to Thursday. The plan mentioned "making bad guesses" and "loitering near people they dislike" — the Freya scene covers the latter, but the former is somewhat absent. The chapter is slightly more competent than the plan intended, which is a minor flaw. Overall, this is confident, well-executed YA fiction with a strong voice, good comic timing, and genuine social specificity. Its weaknesses are minor and mostly involve occasional over-reaching for effect. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
By Wednesday morning, I had managed to make myself look both guilty and unstable, which was efficient even for me. The trouble with having a real problem at school is that you still have to do all the normal humiliating things as well. Hand in essays. Borrow a glue stick. Nod along while someone called Evan explains inflation as if he discovered it personally. The world does not suspend routine just because your old life has started texting. Tasha got the blood-drop message at 1:14. Freya had almost certainly received something physical. I had the note in my blazer pocket and three jelly hearts now slowly furring themselves with lint. Cleo still hadn’t sent a single actual word. I considered going to Mr Firth. Not because I thought he’d fix anything, but because there is a specific sort of panic where adults begin to seem useful by sheer force of not being seventeen. Then I pictured his face if I tried to explain that I was being haunted by an emoji I helped popularise three years ago and chose pride instead. At break, I made the mistake of deciding to confront Ryan without a plan. This was less a decision than a mood. He was by the bike sheds with two of the rugby lot, eating something from the corner shop and doing that thing boys do where they stand around in a triangle pretending not to enjoy being observed. The rain had stopped but the ground was still wet, and the whole yard smelled faintly of leaves and deodorant. “Can I talk to you?” I said. One of his friends made a little ooh noise under his breath, because boys are born in a state of permanent spiritual Year 8. Ryan glanced at them, then at me, and did the mild, patient face that made adults think he was mature. “Yeah, course.” “Alone.” That bought me thirty seconds of silence and one highly interested look from a girl nearby smoking in defiance of all visible futures. Ryan handed his half-eaten flapjack to one of the boys and walked a few steps away with me, not far enough to be private, just far enough to imply decency. “What’s up?” I hadn’t thought beyond this point, which became obvious to both of us at once. “You know exactly what’s up.” His expression shifted by a millimetre. “Do I?” “The messages. The notes. Don’t do that.” “Maeve, I genuinely have no idea what you’re talking about.” There is a way some people say genuinely that should count as perjury. “Really? Three blood drops? Ringing any bells?” He gave a tiny laugh. Not because anything was funny. Because he wanted us both to know he wasn’t rattled. “That old thing?” “Old thing?” “Come on. People have been doing that for ages.” “You’re the one saying people.” “Because it is people. Not me.” There was movement at the edge of my vision. Other students pretending not to look. Of course. School can smell conflict at a quarter mile. “You were there yesterday,” I said. “At the lockers.” “I am at school most days, yes.” “And Thursday. ‘Everyone’ll be here.’” He blinked, still calm. “Sorry, have you been collecting my corridor dialogue now?” The smile that came with it was almost kind. That was the worst thing about Ryan. He had a way of making your own urgency seem socially excessive. Not wrong, exactly. Just embarrassing. “You think this is funny?” I said. “No.” He lowered his voice, which somehow made me feel louder. “I think you’re winding yourself up over something stupid and using me because I’m convenient.” I actually laughed then, once, a nasty little sound. “That is rich.” “Is it?” “Yes.” His eyes sharpened at that. For a second the patient concern slipped, and underneath it was the old thing: not menace, exactly. Irritation. The annoyance of a boy who’d arranged events into a manageable version and didn’t enjoy seeing pieces move. “Look,” he said, “if this is about what happened in summer—” “It is.” “—then I’m not doing this with an audience.” He glanced past me as he said it. I turned. Audience was generous. It was basically half of break. Not everyone. Just enough. Tasha near the wall with a look of pure disbelief. Freya by the doors, frozen. A cluster of Year 12s trying to seem accidental. “You’ve got one anyway,” I said. “Right.” Ryan stepped back. “Then I’m definitely not.” And because school is a machine designed to convert private horror into public farce, that was exactly when Mr Firth appeared from around the corner carrying a stack of photocopies and the expression of a man whose tea has once again gone cold in service of children. “What’s going on here?” Nothing says dignity like being caught in a social detonation by pastoral staff. “Nothing,” Ryan said immediately. “Maeve?” Mr Firth asked. This was the point at which a better person would have lied. Or a smarter one. Instead I said, “Someone’s been sending messages. About summer. And he knows.” Mr Firth looked from me to Ryan and straight away I saw the problem: I was breathing too fast and Ryan looked like a charity volunteer wrongly accused of arson. “What sort of messages?” he said carefully. “Blood drops.” There was a brief silence. Not a dramatic silence. A bad one. “Emoji,” I clarified, because of course I did, making it somehow worse. One of the Year 12s gave a tiny involuntary snort, then pretended to cough himself inside out. Mr Firth shot him a look. “Maeve,” he said, in the tone adults use when they are deciding whether a thing is serious or merely adolescent, “why don’t you come and talk to me properly?” That should have helped. It didn’t. Because Ryan was already shaking his head a little, not theatrically, just enough to imply this was exactly the kind of thing he had been trying to avoid. “I’ve got class,” he said. “I’m happy to come by later if needed.” If needed. Like we were discussing a printer complaint. By lunch, everyone knew some version of it, none of them correct. That I’d accused Ryan of threatening me. That Cleo was back. That there was a sex tape. That there had always been a sex tape. That I’d had some sort of breakdown over fruit pastilles. School gossip is what happens when boredom gets a knife. I ended up in Mr Firth’s office, where he gave me tea in a mug that said IT’S OKAY TO NOT BE OKAY, which felt less comforting than a threat. “I’m not dismissing this,” he said. “You are a bit.” “I’m trying to understand it.” “No, you’re trying to phrase it.” He rubbed his temple. “All right. Then help me understand. Why now? Why would someone send you this now?” Because of 12:07, I thought. Because of Thursday. Because of the thing I did. And then, because apparently humiliation had not yet reached its legal limit, I remembered. The night of the party. Cleo in the corridor, face white with fury, phone in her hand. “Maeve, look at this.” Me already wanting to leave, already hating the way everything had tilted. Music too loud behind us. Someone shouting from the garden. Her trying to show me the video and me seeing enough — enough to know it wasn’t funny, enough to know she wasn’t joking, enough to know I was now being asked to choose. And me smiling. God. Not because it was funny. Because people were there. Because if I’d looked as shocked as I felt, I would have been in it with her. So I’d smiled that awful nervous smile and said, “Cleo, maybe don’t,” like she was the embarrassing one. In Mr Firth’s office, with the tea cooling untouched between us, I went suddenly hot all over. “Maeve?” he said. I looked at him. Then I looked past him, through the office window into the corridor beyond. Cleo was there. Not fully. Not like an apparition. Just a flash as someone turned the corner: dark blonde hair, school coat, head down. By the time I got to the door, she was gone.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 is a strong piece of YA fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Maeve's voice is the chapter's greatest asset. The narration is consistently sharp, self-aware, and genuinely funny without tipping into try-hard territory. Lines like "because boys are born in a state of permanent spiritual Year 8" and "If needed. Like we were discussing a printer complaint" feel earned rather than performed. The internal categorisations, the compulsive noticing, the defensive irony — all consistent with the character profile. The moment where she catches herself smiling at the party is the chapter's emotional core, and it lands because it's specific and ugly in exactly the right way: not a grand moral failure but a precise, cowardly social calculation. This is the chapter plan's central requirement ("the specific thing she did") and it's executed with real skill. Ryan is handled well — the "genuinely" that "should count as perjury," the patient concern that makes urgency seem excessive, the jaw-tightening when rattled. He's recognisably the character described: not a villain, just someone very good at managing his own narrative. The "If needed" line is particularly good characterisation through dialogue. Mr Firth's appearance is darkly comic in exactly the right register — the photocopies, the cold tea, the IT'S OKAY TO NOT BE OKAY mug. He's not cartoonish but he's institutionally useless in the specific way described. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is generally strong. Ryan's lines are calibrated well — the slight condescension, the "genuinely," the "If needed." The exchange has real tension. The "Emoji" clarification moment is a perfect comic beat. The Mr Firth dialogue is slightly more functional than brilliant, but it serves its purpose. The weakness is that some of Maeve's confrontational lines ("You know exactly what's up," "You're the one saying people") feel slightly too clean — real confrontations are messier — but this is a minor complaint. SHOW VS TELL: The chapter mostly shows rather than tells. The flashback to the party is handled through action and sensation rather than explanation, which is the right choice. The "I went suddenly hot all over" is slightly on the nose but acceptable. The gossip list ("That there was a sex tape. That there had always been a sex tape. That I'd had some sort of breakdown over fruit pastilles") is a lovely piece of showing-through-accumulation. PLOT AND STRUCTURE: The chapter follows its plan closely — the wrong confrontation, the public humiliation, the pastoral check-in, the revelation of the specific act of cowardice, and the glimpse of Cleo at the end. The structure is tight. The Cleo appearance at the end is well-placed — not melodramatic, just a flash, which is exactly right for the character and the chapter's tone. METAPHOR AND PROSE: The prose is clean and controlled. There are no purple passages or forced metaphors. The occasional figurative flourish ("school gossip is what happens when boredom gets a knife") is sharp rather than overwrought. The writing doesn't reach for poetry when it shouldn't. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is very competent but occasionally plays it slightly safe. The confrontation with Ryan, while well-written, follows a fairly predictable arc — protagonist goes in without a plan, gets outmaneuvered, looks bad. The chapter plan called for this, but the execution doesn't find many surprises within that structure. The gossip list is funny but the "sex tape" detail feels slightly jarring given the story's careful calibration of the original harm as "filmed humiliation" rather than sexual content — this could be read as the gossip machine distorting things, which is fine, but it's a slightly risky note. The Cleo glimpse at the end is effective but also somewhat expected as a chapter-closing beat. FAITHFULNESS TO PLAN: The chapter hits all its marks — wrong confrontation, public humiliation, pastoral scene, guilt revelation, Cleo sighting. The "setback chapter focused more on character than tidy plot" description is well-served. OVERALL: This is confident, well-crafted YA fiction with a strong voice, good comic timing, and genuine emotional intelligence. It's not quite a masterpiece — it lacks the occasional unexpected turn that would elevate it — but it's consistently good and occasionally excellent. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I left Mr Firth mid-sentence. There may have been consequences to this in a technical sense. If so, they never fully ripened, because pastoral staff are like minor gods of process: slow to wrath and mostly powered by Outlook. I heard him call my name once behind me, but by then I was already out in the corridor, blazer half on, moving too fast. The corridor beyond his office was empty. Not empty in a haunting way. Empty in a school way: a dropped worksheet near the skirting board, one of those institutional display boards about resilience, the smell of floor cleaner failing to defeat feet. The stairwell door was still swinging slightly, though, which was enough to make my stomach kick. I went down the stairs two at a time and nearly collided with a Year 8 carrying a papier-mâché volcano. “Sorry,” I said. He looked at me as if I was a badly supervised horse. Outside, the lower yard was slick with rain. Students were drifting toward lunch in knots and singles. No Cleo. No dramatic silhouette at the gates. Nothing except cold air and my own pulse trying to become public. This is the thing no one tells you about chasing someone you used to know completely: your body recognises them before your certainty does. I knew that glimpse was her in the same way I know my own front door key by touch. Not from logic. From years. Tasha found me five minutes later by the bins behind the drama block, where I was pretending to be there for a reason. “You look appalling,” she said. “Thank you.” “Did you sprint?” “Maybe.” “Why?” “I saw Cleo.” That landed. “Where?” “Outside Firth’s office.” “And?” “She vanished.” Tasha stared at me for a second, then nodded once as if updating some internal chart. “Right. So she’s here.” “Or I’ve finally lost it in a way that’ll make the school newsletter.” “No, if you’d hallucinated her, she’d have made more of an entrance.” That should not have been comforting and somehow was. We spent the rest of lunch doing laps of the likely places Cleo would not go. This is harder than it sounds. If you haven’t spoken to someone in months, your map of them goes stale in odd patches. I knew where Cleo had hidden in Year 10 after crying over a maths test she absolutely did not need to cry over. I knew where she liked to stand before assemblies because the light hit her hair well from the left. I knew which toilet cubicle she used if she wanted privacy and which one if she wanted to be found. But this version of her? The version who texted after midnight and moved through school like a rumour? Less clear. We checked the old music block steps, the side path by the MFL wing, the gap behind the sports hall where sixth formers vape under the wild belief no one can smell mango death. Nothing. At last Tasha said, “Could she be waiting for you to stop doing a perimeter sweep and think?” “That sounds unkind.” “That sounds like Cleo.” She wasn’t wrong. My phone buzzed at 2:13, halfway through history. Unknown number. Library. Top floor. Don’t arrive looking rabid. No signature. None needed. I stared at it until Mr Patel asked whether I planned to contribute to the discussion on interwar appeasement or merely suffer through it silently. Tasha, two seats over, saw my face and mouthed well? I held up the phone under the desk. She looked at the message, then back at me, and wrote on her notes in block capitals: SHE’S SO ANNOYING. Also not wrong. The library top floor is where our school keeps all the books no one wants and all the students staff would rather not supervise closely. It has that particular hush of institutional neglect: warm air, old paper, computers from a previous moral era. I got there straight after the bell and found no one at first except a Year 12 asleep behind a sociology textbook and Mrs Dyer at the desk entering something into a spreadsheet with priestly disappointment. Then a voice, low and dry, from between the biographies and travel. “You took your time.” I turned. Cleo was sitting on the floor in the gap between shelves, knees up, school coat over her uniform, looking both exactly like herself and completely altered by the fact of being there. Her hair was tied back badly. No makeup, or not enough to matter. She looked tired in a way she would once have considered a personal attack. For one unhelpful second all I felt was relief, swift and humiliating. Then I remembered I was angry. “You texted me blood drops at midnight.” “Yes.” “That’s not normal.” She looked up at me. “I’m touched you’ve become such a robust defender of normal.” Even exhausted, she could still do this. Find the line and slide it in. I sat opposite her on the carpet because standing felt like surrender. Between us was a shelf of failed travel writing and one very battered atlas. “You sent them to Tasha too.” “She has initiative.” “And Freya?” A tiny pause. “Possibly.” “Why?” “Because people only become honest when they think a story might start moving without them.” It was such a Cleo answer I nearly laughed. Instead I said, “Are you back?” “In school? No. In the spiritual sense, perhaps.” “Can you not.” “No, apparently I can’t.” There she was. The old rhythm, intact enough to be dangerous. Up close, though, she looked thinner. Not dramatically. Just less arranged. There was a healing scratch by her thumb where she’d clearly picked at the skin. Cleo had always been very still in a social way, all held lines and controlled angles. Now she seemed to be using that same control simply to remain in one piece. “I saw you in September,” I said before I meant to. “The screenshot.” “Of course you did.” “You could’ve just told me.” That got me a look so flat it should have taken wallpaper off. “I did tell you,” she said. “In August. In the corridor. With my actual mouth.” I swallowed. There was no point denying it. Not with her. Not with the exact memory still sitting in me like glass. “I know.” “No, you know now. At the time you decided to become very interested in not making things worse.” “You think I don’t know what I did?” “I think you know now because it’s come back for you. Which is not quite the same.” There are arguments where the air itself starts choosing sides. This was one. I said, “Why text me at all?” At that, something in her shifted. Not softened. Focused. “Because he thinks it’s over,” she said. “And because he’s wrong.” From inside her coat pocket she took out her phone. Cracked screen, old case, still the ridiculous green nail varnish stain in one corner from Year 12 mocks. She unlocked it and held it where I could see. On the screen was a video. Not the screenshot. The actual clip. Grainy corridor. Music from the hall bleeding through the walls. A cluster of people just out of frame laughing in that sharp, anticipatory way groups do before someone gets hurt. Cleo in the middle of it, clearly not in on the joke. Ryan’s voice, low and amused, saying, “No, wait, just let her see—” Then the camera jerking as if whoever filmed it was trying not to be obvious. Cleo reaching for the phone. Her own face changing as she realises what’s already been sent, and to whom. Not sexy. Not scandalous. Worse. Humiliating. Designed to look consensual if you already wanted it to be. I felt sick. “He still has copies,” Cleo said. “Or had. And now, conveniently, he wants everything gone before open evening.” “Why open evening?” “Because his mum’s helping. Because staff will be distracted. Because if it leaks then, he can say someone’s being malicious for attention.” She looked at me steadily. “And because Freya was there. She saw more than she’s saying.” I looked from the phone to her face. “Why me?” Cleo’s laugh was tiny and awful. “Because, Maeve, you remember things properly. And because if this comes out, your part comes with it.” There it was. Not trust. Usefulness. Fair enough. She locked the screen. “I need somewhere to leave a copy.” I said, “With me?” “With someone I know won’t accidentally grow a conscience and hand it to a safeguarding form.” “That’s an incredibly flattering way to phrase blackmail.” “It’s not blackmail.” She stood up, brushing dust from her skirt. “It’s delegation.” Then, after a beat: “Thursday,” she said. “Don’t make me ask twice.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of YA fiction that largely delivers on its plan and the broader story's intentions. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically. VOICE AND CHARACTER Maeve's voice is consistently rendered throughout. The defensive intelligence, the self-lacerating observation, the compulsive noticing — all present and functioning. Lines like "pastoral staff are like minor gods of process: slow to wrath and mostly powered by Outlook" are genuinely funny and feel earned rather than performed. The categorisation of the empty corridor as "empty in a school way" rather than "empty in a haunting way" is exactly the kind of deflating specificity the plan called for. The voice doesn't slip into generic YA narration at any point. Cleo's characterisation is the chapter's greatest achievement. She arrives exhausted, diminished, and still capable of the precise verbal strike. The exchange about "you know now because it's come back for you" is the chapter's emotional core, and it lands because it's specific and unflattering to both parties.
