This is partly inherent to the setup, but the plan promised something "jagged" and this chapter is quite smooth. The ending is effective but slightly conventional—the three hard raps is a well-worn thriller beat. The reflection-in-glass reveal is good, but the final line ("Not tentative. Not neighborly.") is the kind of thing that tells us what we've already understood. The class texture is present but could be sharper. The "almost nothing in the kitchen you could eat without assembly" observation is good; some of the other wealth-signaling (brass fixtures, bowl of limes) is competent but not distinctive. **Faithfulness to Plan** The chapter follows the plan very closely—perhaps too closely in places, as it reads like a careful execution of a checklist rather than a story that discovered itself in the writing. But all the required elements are present and handled with skill. Overall, this is a confident, well-crafted opening chapter that establishes character, atmosphere, and threat efficiently. Its weaknesses are mostly of degree rather than kind: slightly too smooth, occasionally reaching for a metaphor that doesn't quite earn its place, and ending on a beat that's effective but familiar. It's professional-quality pulp fiction with genuine literary ambition in the characterization. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Rita put one finger to her lips without looking at Ava. The knock came again. Three hard, patient raps. The kind a man used when he thought he already owned the answer. “Take your radio,” Rita said quietly. “Come with me.” Ava slid off the stool. No questions, which was better than bravery. Rita moved her through the mudroom into the laundry room, where louvered doors hid the storm closet under the back stair. She’d clocked it on the first pass: bottled water, blankets, lantern, shelf of board games no one had opened. Rich people loved preparing for disasters as décor. Inside, the air smelled of detergent and drywall. “You stay here,” Rita said. “No noise unless the house is on fire and you can see it. If I tell you run, you run. If somebody says they’re police, you wait until you hear me say otherwise. You understand?” Ava held the orange radio against her chest. “What if you don’t say anything?” “Then you stay put until you hear me or until morning.” “That’s stupid.” “Most survival is.” The knock sounded a third time, louder. Ava looked at her. “Are they here for me?” Rita saw fear under the flatness now. Real and trying not to be. “I don’t know yet,” she said. “But they’re not getting you easy.” That landed. Ava gave one sharp nod. Rita pulled the closet door mostly shut, leaving it just unlatched. Then she crossed the laundry room, took the long way back through the mudroom, and picked up Celia Mercer’s abandoned tablet from the kitchen island. The screen lit under her thumb. No passcode, because rich people trusted walls and poor people to make up for their carelessness. The security app was already open. Useful. She flicked through camera feeds. Front drive: empty except for the gleam of wet concrete. Side yard: one broad man in a baseball cap, one lean one with a shaved head and a gray polo plastered dark at the armpits. Back patio: no one. The dead breakfast-nook camera remained dead. The broad one smiled at the lens and lifted a hand, friendly as a salesman. The back door handle moved. Locked. Good. Rita set the tablet on the counter, took the chef’s knife from the butcher block, thought better of it, and put it back. Knives were arguments. She wanted leverage. She slid open the junk drawer beside the stove, found a heavy flashlight and a long grill lighter. Better. Then she killed the kitchen lights. The room folded into reflections and storm-gray. The broad man knocked again, then tried the handle harder. “Mrs. Mercer? Mr. Mercer said to come by.” His voice carried through the glass in a lazy local drawl. Comfortable. Fake. Rita stayed out of sight. “Mr. Mercer can call me.” A beat. “Ma’am, our phones aren’t connecting. He asked us to pick up a package.” “Then he can call me.” The men looked at each other. The lean one scanned the dark kitchen, his posture changing first. He knew enough to dislike what he couldn’t see. “Lady,” the broad one said, smile gone from his voice, “open the door.” Rita touched the lock and, instead of opening it, hit the patio lights. Sudden white flooded the concrete and made them blink. “Try a different house,” she said. The broad one grinned now because he thought this was the part where women started negotiating. “Don’t make this annoying.” “Too late.” He stepped back from the door. Rita moved before the glass burst. The first hit took the lower pane in a blossom of safety cubes. The broad man came through shoulder-first, using the broken frame like he’d done it before. The lean one reached in to unlock from inside. Rita slammed the mudroom door between kitchen and hall and cut sideways as the broad man planted a boot on tile. He expected a woman in his chest, maybe a scratch. He did not expect the full Maglite whipped two-handed into the side of his knee. Bone made a wet cracking pop. He folded with a scream more offended than pained. Rita hit him again in the mouth. Teeth clicked. Blood sprayed the white fridge. The lean man swore and came through low and fast. He had a knife this time, small and practical. Rita threw the flashlight at his face, not to hurt him but to make him blink, and backed into the pantry as he lunged. He followed, because men followed. She yanked the door hard by the brass knob. It caught his knife hand wrist-deep in the jamb. He made a sound like a bark. Rita leaned all her weight into the door and heard the small bright grind of finger bones going wrong. He dropped the knife. She opened just enough to grab his wrist and twisted until he hit the floor on one shoulder. He looked up at her, shocked now. “Jesus Christ—” “Not tonight,” Rita said, and stamped his elbow. It gave. He howled. The broad man was up again in the kitchen on one good leg, face blood-slick and blank with rage. He’d pulled a handgun from the back of his waistband like this had finally become the kind of evening he preferred. Rita snatched the long grill lighter from the counter and triggered the gas burner as she moved. Blue flame leaped. She grabbed the skillet Celia had left drying, still greasy from somebody’s reheated dinner, and flung the hot oil and pan together. Mostly steam. A spit of grease. Enough. The broad man threw up an arm, gun wobbling as heat and oil hit his cheek and eye. He fired once into the ceiling. Drywall dust rained. Rita crossed the distance before he had his balance and drove the edge of her hand into his throat, not delicate, just accurate. He gagged, stumbled back into the island, and she took his gun wrist in both hands and smashed it against the marble corner. The crack this time was clean. He dropped the gun and made a sound from some older part of himself. Rita kicked it under the dishwasher. For one second there was only breathing. The kitchen stank of hot grease, blood, and gas. Then the broad man looked at her with tears in his ruined eye and said, “What the fuck are you?” Rita, breathing hard now, felt the old cold room inside herself opening by degrees. “The babysitter,” she said. From the laundry room wing came the thump of something small striking a wall. Ava, hearing too much. The lean man dragged himself partly upright with his good arm, face gray. “Mick said she’d be alone.” Broad Man spat blood. “Call him.” Rita moved toward him and he flinched, which told her everything useful. Instead of hitting him she took his phone from his shirt pocket. Screen already lit. Four missed calls from LEON K. Interesting. She answered the next one and said nothing. A man’s voice came on smooth as a hand down a dog’s back. “Well?” Rita looked at the two men bleeding on the Mercer tile. Rain began at last, hard enough to sound like thrown gravel on the windows. “Your boys are making a mess,” she said. Silence. Then: “Who is this?” The broad man closed his eyes. Rita smiled without humor. “Worse,” she said, and hung up.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 delivers on nearly every promise made in the planning document. The execution is tight, confident, and specific in the ways the plan demanded. Let me work through the key elements critically. VOICE AND STYLE: The prose is muscular and economical without being stripped to the point of sterility. Lines like "The kind a man used when he thought he already owned the answer" establish Rita's observational intelligence without over-explaining it. The rhythm is controlled. The chapter moves fast but not sloppily. There are no wasted paragraphs. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Rita behaves exactly as profiled. She checks the security app, uses the house's infrastructure rather than brute force, keeps her head, and deploys violence that is practical and bodily specific rather than action-hero choreography. The detail of her choosing the flashlight over the knife ("Knives were arguments. She wanted leverage") is excellent character work compressed into two sentences. Ava's behavior in the closet is consistent with her profile: no questions initially, then a sharp practical objection ("What if you don't say anything?"), then the fear breaking through the flatness. The exchange "Are they here for me?" / "I don't know yet. But they're not getting you easy." is exactly the kind of honest, non-sentimental reassurance the character profile called for. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is largely strong. The intruder's "Mr. Mercer said to come by" and Rita's repeated "Then he can call me" establishes her control of the exchange efficiently. "What the fuck are you?" / "The babysitter" is the chapter's best beat—earned, not quippy, landing with the weight of everything that preceded it. The lean man's "Mick said she'd be alone" is a clean exposition delivery that doesn't feel forced. The final phone exchange is excellent: "Your boys are making a mess" / "Who is this?" / "Worse" lands the chapter's closing note with real punch. ACTION SEQUENCE: The fight choreography is the chapter's most impressive achievement. It is ugly, intimate, and specific. The pantry door crushing the lean man's hand, the skillet grease and steam, the wrist broken on marble—these feel like things that would actually happen in a kitchen fight, not movie-fight beats. The broad man's gun going off into the ceiling from pain and surprise is exactly right. Rita's bad knee is not mentioned but her use of leverage and positioning rather than strength is consistent with someone who fights smart because she has to. WEAKNESSES: A few minor issues. "Blue flame leaped" is slightly generic. "The old cold room inside herself opening by degrees" is the chapter's one moment of purple-adjacent prose—it's not terrible, but it's the most self-conscious line in an otherwise unselfconscious chapter. The Maglite detail is slightly convenient (she just happens to find it in the junk drawer alongside the grill lighter), though this is a minor quibble. The chapter's pacing is so relentless that the emotional texture between Rita and Ava, while present, is necessarily compressed. The plan called for their rapport to "begin with irritation and sharpen into trust"—we get the beginning of that, but the chapter is so action-focused that character development is necessarily thin. This is appropriate for the chapter's function, not a failure. FOLLOWING THE PLAN: The chapter follows its plan closely and well. The men claim to be sent by Mercer, Rita doesn't buy it, Ava goes to the storm closet with the radio, the intruders come in anyway, Rita uses the house itself (lights, security app, pantry door, skillet), the violence is ugly and intimate, and the chapter ends with the phone call establishing a bigger threat. The plan's note that "one intruder on the phone to someone bigger: 'She's not alone. No. Worse.'" is delivered almost verbatim, which is fine because it's a good beat. CREATIVITY: The chapter avoids generic thriller beats consistently. The pantry door as a weapon is genuinely inventive. The security app used offensively (patio lights to blind them) is smart. The phone call reversal at the end is clean. OVERALL: This is a very strong chapter of pulp fiction with genuine craft behind it. It delivers on its promises, maintains voice, keeps characters consistent, and executes its action sequence with specificity and intelligence. The weaknesses are minor and mostly inherent to the chapter's function as an action set piece. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 17 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The phone started ringing again before Rita set it down. She muted it and looked at the two men on the kitchen floor. Broad One sat against the island cradling his shattered wrist and making a wet whistling sound every time he breathed. Lean One had dragged himself half into the pantry doorway, his broken arm clutched to his chest like a punished thing. “Names,” Rita said. Broad One glared through one watering eye. She crouched, picked up his ruined hand, and squeezed just enough. He screamed. “Denny,” he gasped. She looked at the other one. “Cal,” he said quickly. “Jesus, lady—” “Not a lady.” Rita let Denny’s hand go. “What did Mick send you for?” They exchanged a look. Reflex. Hierarchy. Rita stood and turned the stove off. “I can keep doing this until one of you gets practical.” Cal swallowed, face slick and gray. “A card.” “What kind of card?” “Memory card. Tiny thing. Mercer took it.” “From where?” Denny spat blood onto Celia Mercer’s white grout. “From Mick.” “Helpful,” Rita said. “Why send housebreakers instead of a phone call?” Denny laughed once and then coughed around the broken shape of his throat. “Because Dan Mercer stopped answering his phone.” Lightning flashed blue-white through the windows. The house answered a second later with a long low shudder of thunder. From the laundry room came the smallest sound in the world: a floorboard settling under a child trying not to exist. Rita heard it. So did Cal. His eyes flicked that direction by reflex. There it was. Rita didn’t move fast enough to stop the glance, but she moved fast enough to make him regret it. She caught his jaw in one hand and drove his head into the pantry trim. He slumped. “Wrong direction,” she said. The back door shattered inward. Not another dramatic entrance this time. A key in the lock. A man who knew houses. Deputy Leon Kearse stepped through the broken lower pane with rain on his shoulders and one hand lifted away from his holster in a parody of calm. “Easy,” he said. “Everybody easy.” He wore plain clothes under a rain shell, badge clipped at his belt where a decent man might put a phone. Square face, trimmed beard, eyes taking inventory too quickly for any actual surprise. Behind him, through the rain, Rita saw another silhouette hanging back under the eaves. Kearse’s gaze landed on Denny, then Cal, then on Rita standing barefoot in rich people’s kitchen light with a flashlight on the floor and blood on her forearm. “Ma’am,” he said. “You want to tell me what happened here?” Rita laughed softly. Something shifted in his expression. Not much. Just enough. “Get out,” she said. “Now let’s take a breath.” “Take yours outside.” He stepped one pace farther into the kitchen, careful not to cut his shoes on the glass. “I got a call about a disturbance. These men say Mr. Mercer hired them to collect personal property and you attacked them.” Denny, to his credit or stupidity, nodded. Rita looked at Kearse. “And you believed that because you’re an idiot or because this is yours too?” Kearse smiled with half his mouth. “I think you’re upset.” “Good catch.” The silhouette outside moved. Bigger than Kearse. Waiting. Rita saw Kearse’s eyes tick past her left shoulder toward the mudroom, too casual to be anything but deliberate. They knew or guessed there was somebody else in the house. “Child upstairs?” he asked lightly. “No child of mine.” “Then let’s make sure she’s safe.” He took another step. Rita bent as if to pick up the dropped flashlight. Instead she snatched Broad Denny’s gun from under the dishwasher with her other hand and came up aiming center mass. Kearse froze, more offended than scared. “Now that,” he said, “is a very bad choice.” “For who?” Rain hammered the windows harder. Somewhere in the house a smoke alarm chirped once from the shot in the ceiling and then thought better of it. Kearse’s face settled into something gentler and uglier. “Listen to me. Mick just wants his property back. The little girl’s father got stupid. You don’t have a dog in this hunt. Hand over the card, hand over the kid, and you get to go home.” There it was, plain enough to live on. From the laundry room came Ava’s voice, small and sharp as broken glass. “I don’t know where it is.” Kearse’s eyes shifted. Rita wanted to kill Cal for that glance all over again. “Sweetheart,” Kearse called, warm now, “nobody’s mad at you. Why don’t you come out and we sort this out?” Rita fired into the marble island beside his hand. The blast in the kitchen was enormous. Stone chips stung his cheek. Kearse jerked back, all the fake patience stripped clean off him. “I said outside.” For a second she thought he might draw. Then he looked at Denny gasping on the floor, at Cal half-conscious, at Rita holding steady, and made the calculation a smaller man would call restraint. He lifted both hands. “All right.” He backed through the broken doorway into the rain. The second silhouette followed him down the patio. Rita locked the deadbolt behind them out of habit, though habit wasn’t going to stop what came next. “Ava,” she said. “Out. Now.” Ava emerged with the orange weather radio and a face gone paper-white. Her eyes dropped once to Denny’s hand and then snapped away. “Did you kill them?” “Not yet.” Rita took the radio from her. “You lied.” Ava hugged herself. “I didn’t know if you were with them.” “That answer gets one point for honesty and loses several for timing.” Rita popped the back off the radio. Batteries. Under them, wrapped in clear tape, a thumbnail-sized memory card. Of course. Ava stared. “You found it fast.” “You picked the one object in this house you actually care about.” Outside, tires hissed on wet concrete. More than one vehicle. Rita slid the card into her jeans pocket. “What’s on it?” “My dad took it from his jacket and then put it back and then I took it because he was acting weird.” The words came clipped and flat now. “He told my mom if Mick had it they were all dead, and she said we already were.” Good families, Rita thought. America’s best. Headlights swung across the side yard. Through the window she saw at least two SUVs nosing into the drive. “We’re leaving,” she said. “How?” Rita glanced at the tablet. Security feed still live on some cameras. Front drive full of white glare and rain. Side gate to the neighboring build lot still latched. Beyond that, the skeleton of a model home under wraps. “This house have attic access?” Ava pointed. “Hall closet.” “Grab shoes.” Ava looked down at her bare feet as if surprised to discover them. “Right.” They moved fast. Rita took the tablet, the flashlight, and the gun. She kicked Denny’s phone under the stove, then thought better and pocketed it too. Information weighed nothing. As they hit the hall, pounding sounded from the front door. Not knocking now. Workman’s blows. In the closet, pull-cord stairs dropped with a squeal. Rita boosted Ava up first, bad knee shouting, then followed into insulation heat and rafters that smelled of hot wood and dust. Below them the front door gave. Men’s voices. Kearse’s lower than the others. Rita crawled toward the rear vent hatch, Ava ahead of her on silent hands and knees, radio slung crossbody like a tiny soldier’s kit. “You ever been in the house next door?” Rita whispered. “The one that isn’t finished?” “Yes.” “There’s no drywall on the second floor.” “Perfect.” She shoved the vent screen out with the flashlight butt. Rain blew in, warm and hard. The drop to the narrow side roof was ugly but survivable. Behind them, below, a voice called: “Ava? We know you’re in here.” Ava closed her eyes. Rita put a hand between her shoulder blades. “Move.” They went out into the storm.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes its plan with considerable skill and discipline. The model has delivered a tight, propulsive siege chapter that transitions into escape, hitting nearly every beat from the outline while maintaining a consistent voice and avoiding most of the pitfalls it identified in its own planning notes. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is genuinely good. Lines like "Not a lady," "Good catch," and "For who?" are economical and character-revealing without being quippy. Kearse's "Now that is a very bad choice" and Rita's "For who?" exchange is a small masterpiece of compressed menace and counter-menace. The dialogue doesn't over-explain; it reveals hierarchy, threat, and character through what people choose to say and not say. Kearse's fake warmth when he calls to Ava ("Sweetheart, nobody's mad at you") is exactly the right register of predatory calm the character profile demands. Rita's characterization is consistent with the profile throughout. She is methodical, cold, practical, and capable of violence without theatrical flourish. The detail of her snatching the gun from under the dishwasher while pretending to reach for the flashlight is the kind of specific, earned competence that avoids the "former special forces" trap. Her bad knee gets a mention during the attic climb. She pockets Denny's phone because "information weighed nothing" — a small, perfect character beat. Ava is rendered well. Her voice is flat under pressure, her explanation of the card is clipped and procedural ("He told my mom if Mick had it they were all dead, and she said we already were"), and her hiding the card in the radio battery compartment fits her established personality exactly. The line "You picked the one object in this house you actually care about" is one of the chapter's best — it shows Rita's observational intelligence and the developing dynamic between them without sentimentality. The pacing is excellent. The chapter moves from interrogation to Kearse's arrival to standoff to escape without wasted motion. The storm is used atmospherically without being over-described. The violence is quick and bodily — squeezing a shattered wrist, driving a head into trim — rather than choreographed. The class texture is present but not belabored: "Denny spat blood onto Celia Mercer's white grout" is a single image that does real work. WEAKNESSES: The line "small and sharp as broken glass" for Ava's voice is the chapter's most obvious reach for poetry, and it's slightly strained — broken glass isn't particularly sharp-sounding, it's sharp-edged. It's a minor lapse in an otherwise clean prose register. "Good families, Rita thought. America's best." is a dry internal observation that works, but it's the kind of line that could tip into self-congratulatory noir if overused. Here it's earned, but it's worth noting. The chapter follows the plan closely but perhaps too closely in one respect: the revelation of the card's location and contents is handled efficiently but slightly mechanically. Ava's explanation ("He told my mom if Mick had it they were all dead") is functional but the emotional weight of that revelation — a child summarizing her parents' terror — could have landed harder with one more beat of silence or reaction from Rita. The "Not yet" response to "Did you kill them?" is the chapter's one moment that edges toward the quippy action-hero register the plan explicitly wanted to avoid. It's a small thing, but it's the kind of line that belongs in a different, more self-consciously cool story. The chapter plan called for Rita to "get the first clue that this is about Mercer and something hidden in the house" — this is handled, but the transition from "first clue" to "full understanding" happens very quickly. The plan suggested this revelation would be more gradual, but the chapter compresses it efficiently, which is a reasonable creative choice even if it slightly undercuts the discovery's weight. OVERALL: This is strong, professional pulp fiction writing. The prose is clean and muscular, the characters are consistent, the plot moves, and the darkness is earned rather than performed. It's not a masterpiece — there are small lapses and one or two moments where the writing reaches slightly beyond its grasp — but it's genuinely good work that delivers on its promises. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The rain hit like thrown gravel and then like hands. Rita slid first, boots skidding on the slick pitch of the side roof, one palm braced, the other gripping the gutter. Ava came after her on all fours with the orange radio bumping against her ribs. Below them, side-yard lanterns cut white cones through the downpour. Men moved in and out of them like actors in a terrible play. “Don’t look down,” Rita said. “I wasn’t,” Ava lied. The unfinished house next door stood twenty feet away across a strip of mud and stacked pavers, wrapped in torn housewrap that snapped and slithered in the wind. The side gate between properties had never been latched properly; Rita had noticed that too. She dropped first, hanging from the gutter and letting herself fall into wet hibiscus mulch with a jolt all the way up her bad knee. Pain flared mean and bright. She breathed through it. “Jump.” Ava did. Rita caught her awkwardly around the waist and almost went over backward. For a second they were both in the flowerbed, mud and leaves and rainwater in their mouths. From inside the Mercer house a man shouted, “Back side! Go!” Rita hauled Ava up and shoved her toward the gate. It stuck halfway. Rita kicked it open. Metal screamed. They ran bent low through a side yard of fresh sod and construction trash, into the skeletal dark of the model home next door. No windows yet on the second floor. No drywall. Just studs, wiring, damp plywood, and the smell of wet lumber. They climbed because height bought seconds. On the raw subfloor upstairs, rain hissing through open cutouts, Rita took Denny’s phone from her pocket and the memory card from the radio. Her fingers were slick. She swore under her breath, wiped them on her shirt, and shoved the card into the phone. “Can you—” “Maybe.” Ava stood beside her, soaked hair plastered to her face, trying not to shake. Down below, flashlights skimmed through the framed first floor next door. Voices scattered. Kearse’s came once, calm and carrying: “She’s got the girl. Watch the lot line.” The phone recognized the card after a second that felt rude. A list of files appeared. Videos. PDFs. Images. Dates. One thumbnail loaded automatically before Rita could stop it: a body half-buried in churned earth at a construction site, one arm visible, a work glove still on. Ava inhaled sharply. Rita clicked away before more came up. Enough. More than enough. “What is it?” Ava asked. “Proof.” “Of what?” “That your father was keeping insurance.” Ava looked down into the rain-dark studs below them as if her father might materialize there and explain himself. “Is he dead?” “I don’t know.” “You keep saying that.” “Because I don’t know.” That seemed to offend her more than a lie would have. A siren sounded somewhere in the distance. Not close. Not for them. Just weather or highway or somebody else’s bad night. Rita checked the files again, scanning names. Doran Holdings. Parish fill permits. Shell invoices. Video clips. One audio file labeled K 6/11. Another PDF with Leon Kearse’s name in a reimbursement chain too dumb to be real. Bought and paid for. Fine. A flashlight beam knifed across the open lower floor. Someone had come into the model home. Rita pocketed the phone and card. “We’re moving.” “To where?” “That depends how many friends your deputy has.” They went down the back side of the frame and out through plastic sheeting into the drainage ditch behind the lots. Water was already running there shin-deep, warm and fast, carrying grass clippings, Styrofoam cups, somebody’s escaped sandal. Ava made a sound of disgust and stepped in anyway. Bell Street was all wrong in storm-light. The pretty houses sat with their uplighting and shutters while runoff swallowed the curbs. Rita cut behind them, through utility easements and strips of ornamental grass, keeping to the dark between pools of motion light. Ava stayed close without needing to be told now. At the end of the block Rita stopped behind a transformer box and looked back. White headlights turned slow at the Mercers’ house. Another vehicle cruised the cross street too deliberately. No easy exits. “You got anybody local who’d come get you if your mother called crying?” Rita asked. Ava gave her a look. “No.” “Good.” “That’s not good.” “It is tonight.” They made it to Rita’s car by luck and geography. She’d parked two streets over under a live oak because rich developments hated curb clutter. The old Civic looked indecent among the wet Mercedes and tall trucks. Rita got them in, locked the doors, and sat for one beat with both hands on the wheel, rain drumming the roof. “You’re bleeding,” Ava said. Rita looked down. Shallow slice along her forearm, probably from the broken glass. “Still inside the body. Fine.” “Where are we going?” Rita started the car. “I’m deciding.” The obvious choices lined up and died one by one. Police station: Kearse. Emergency room: cameras, forms, cops. Motel: names and receipts. Any friend from her current life: too close, too soft. If Doran had local reach, which he did, then every respectable door was a slow trap. She drove toward town anyway, wipers slapping. Bell Street gave way to the county road, then to a strip of chain stores and gas stations shining ugly in rain. At the first red light she called the sheriff’s non-emergency line from Denny’s phone and said, “I need to report a home invasion at the Mercer property on Bell Street. Armed men. One identifies himself as Deputy Kearse.” The dispatcher paused exactly one beat too long. “Ma’am, units are already on scene.” “Are they?” “Yes, ma’am. The situation is under control.” Rita hung up. Ava had heard enough. “So no one’s coming.” “No one useful.” They drove past the emergency room entrance anyway. Fluorescent awning, people smoking in scrubs, an ambulance backing in with no lights. Safe enough for ordinary miseries. Not for this. At a gas station Rita bought two bottles of water, chips, and a prepaid phone with cash. She left Ava locked in the car and watched her in the reflection the whole time. Inside the station, the television behind the counter played radar over cheerful music. Outside, everything smelled like wet asphalt and diesel. Back in the car she handed Ava the chips. “I’m not hungry.” “Eat anyway.” Ava obeyed after a second, taking tiny furious bites. Rita drove toward the older part of town where streets had names instead of branding and live oaks bent over cinderblock churches. Her phone buzzed once. Unknown number. Then again. Then a text from Celia Mercer at last. IS AVA WITH YOU No hello. No apology. No where are you. Rich people saved panic for when it might cost them. Rita typed back: Yes. Three dots. Then: KEEP HER HIDDEN. DON’T TRUST ANYONE FROM COUNTY. A laugh escaped Rita before she could stop it. Ava looked over. “What?” “Your mother finally got brave enough to be useful by text.” Another light. Another hiss of tires through standing water. Rita thought of all the hard, official places in this county she could be funneled if she chose wrong. There was one place not hard, not official, and exactly why men like Doran would overlook it. “You religious?” she asked. Ava frowned. “No.” “Good. Makes the pitch easier.” “Where are we going?” Rita turned onto a narrow street lined with old houses, a bait shop gone dark, and the low brick shape of Saint Bartholomew’s annex hall with its storm shutters folded open. “To a church,” she said. “Try not to look thrilled.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents strong, confident pulp fiction writing that largely delivers on its plan and character profiles. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Craft and Voice** The prose is lean and purposeful. The opening "Rain hit like thrown gravel and then like hands" is a good, earned simile—it escalates in a way that feels intentional rather than decorative. The chapter maintains a tight third-person close on Rita throughout, with dry observations that feel character-specific rather than generically noir. "Still inside the body. Fine." is exactly the kind of line the character profile promised. The voice is consistent and controlled. The chapter avoids the trap of inflated noir cosplay the plan warned against. When it reaches for imagery, it's usually grounded: "actors in a terrible play" is a bit generic, but "somebody's escaped sandal" in the drainage ditch is specific and good. The gas station television playing radar over cheerful music is a nice environmental detail. **Character Consistency** Rita behaves exactly as profiled: checking the phone files with clinical efficiency, refusing to perform emotion, feeding Ava chips with a terse command, noticing the bleeding only when pointed out. Her laugh at Celia's text is perfectly calibrated—contemptuous but not cartoonish. The line "Rich people saved panic for when it might cost them" is Rita's voice precisely. Ava is well-rendered. Her lie about not looking down, her tiny furious bites of chips, her flat "No" to the religious question—these feel like the specific, difficult child described in the profile rather than a generic precocious kid. "Adults always say calm down like it's a button somewhere" isn't in this chapter but the spirit of it is: "You keep saying that. Because I don't know. That seemed to offend her more than a lie would have." That's a sharp, specific observation about this particular child. **Following the Chapter Plan** The plan called for: emergency room parking lot, police station drive-by, a call to an old nurse friend, establishing no safe institutions, Kearse's false narrative, Ava confirming her father's fear, Rita deciding not to hand Ava to anyone local, and ending at the church with Eunice. The chapter hits most of these beats but compresses or omits some. The "call to an old nurse friend" doesn't appear. The police station drive-by is replaced by a phone call to dispatch, which is actually more efficient and revealing. Ava confirming her father's fear is handled obliquely—she asks if he's dead, which implies it without the explicit confirmation the plan described. The chapter ends arriving at the church but Eunice hasn't appeared yet, which is a slight deviation (the plan suggests leaving Ava with Eunice at chapter's end). The final line about the card's contents and Rita deciding to go after Doran is absent—the chapter ends on arrival at the church rather than with Rita's decision to hunt. This is the most significant deviation from the plan. **Dialogue** The dialogue is functional and character-specific without being showy. "You're bleeding." / "Still inside the body. Fine." is excellent. The dispatcher exchange is efficient and chilling. The Celia text exchange is well-handled. The chapter avoids over-explaining through dialogue. One slight weakness: "To where?" / "That depends how many friends your deputy has" is a bit of a quip, slightly too smooth for the moment, but it's minor. **Plot and Tension** The escape sequence is credible and physical without being action-movie slick. The discovery of the thumbnail image (body in construction site) is well-placed and appropriately horrifying without being dwelt upon. The chapter maintains tension through geography and decision-making rather than set pieces. The dispatcher's "one beat too long" pause is economical and effective. **Weaknesses** The chapter's main weakness is that it ends slightly before its planned destination—Rita and Ava arrive at the church but the chapter doesn't deliver the Eunice introduction or Rita's decision to go hunting, which the plan specified as the chapter's culminating beat. This makes the chapter feel slightly truncated at the end, like it stops rather than lands. The final image (turning onto the narrow street) is decent but not as strong as the plan's intended ending with Rita deciding "very simply, to go make him start losing things." Some of the escape sequence is slightly generic—the drainage ditch, the model home, the flashlight beams—though it's executed competently. The class texture is present but could be sharper in places. The line "Bell Street gave way to the county road, then to a strip of chain stores and gas stations shining ugly in rain" is good. "Shining ugly" is a nice compression. **Overall Assessment** This is accomplished genre writing with a distinctive voice, consistent characters, and controlled prose. It's not a masterpiece—it doesn't quite reach the emotional depth the plan aspired to in terms of deepening the Rita/Ava relationship, and it ends slightly short of its planned destination. But it's genuinely good: specific, taut, and free of the amateurish tics that plague lesser pulp fiction. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Saint Bartholomew’s annex hall sat two blocks from the bayou behind a chain-link fence and a row of crape myrtles that had survived more storms than most marriages. In daylight it looked tired but serviceable. In the storm, with the parking lot half-flooded and the sodium lights buzzing, it looked like the sort of place people remembered only when everything else had failed. Eunice Bell opened the side door before Rita knocked twice. She wore church slacks, compression stockings, and a gray cardigan over a T-shirt that said JESUS LOVES YOU, EVERYBODY ELSE THINKS YOU’RE A PROBLEM. Reading glasses hung on a chain at her chest. Her hair was wrapped in a silk scarf the color of plums. “Well,” she said, taking in Rita’s wet clothes, the blood on her sleeve, and Ava at her side. “If you brought trouble in here, at least wipe its feet.” “No time,” Rita said. “Need the shelter room for a few hours.” Eunice’s eyes flicked to Ava, then back. “Child yours?” “No.” “Mm. Better chance she lives, then. Come on.” The annex smelled like old coffee, floor wax, and canned green beans from the pantry closet. Eunice led them past a bulletin board of curling flyers and into the interior room they used during hurricane season, a cinderblock rectangle with folding cots, battery lanterns, and a mural of Noah’s ark that looked less reassuring the older you got. “Sit,” Eunice said to Ava. “You need dry socks?” Ava, too tired now for suspicion to do all the work, nodded once. Eunice handed her a church-lost-and-found sweatshirt with a retreat logo and disappeared toward the office. Rita checked the room on instinct: one door, one tiny reinforced window, heavy cabinet, no second exit. Bad if found. Decent if not. Ava sat on a cot and peeled off wet sneakers. “Is she always like that?” “Yes.” “I like her.” “Your standards are improving.” Ava changed into dry socks and the huge sweatshirt, swallowing herself in it. She looked suddenly younger. Rita hated that. Childhood looked worst when it returned at the wrong time. Eunice came back with towels, a first-aid kit, and a mug of coffee she handed to Rita without asking. “Office line’s been ringing twice and stopping,” she said. “That means somebody thinks they know where to look and isn’t sure who’s home.” Rita nodded. “County dirty.” “County was dirty when I still had knees worth bragging on.” Eunice glanced at Ava. “You got names?” “Mick Doran. Deputy Leon Kearse.” Eunice made a face like she’d bitten foil. “Mm. That kind of dirt.” Rita cleaned the cut on her forearm at the sink in the corner while Ava ate saltines from the storm supplies and watched everything. The water in the basin ran pink and then clear. “What did your father do?” Rita asked without turning. Ava crunched, swallowed. “Business.” “Not a real answer.” “It’s the one he used.” Rita looked at her in the mirror over the sink. “Try again.” Ava picked at a thread on the sweatshirt cuff. “He was scared all week. He yelled at my mom in the pantry because he thought I couldn’t hear through the vent. He said Mick would bury all of them if the files got out. She said he should have thought of that before he got greedy.” Children in rich houses, Rita thought, always heard through vents. “You saw him hide the card?” “He had his jacket on the mud bench. The pocket was ripped. I took it after he left. I thought maybe if I had something, somebody would tell me what was happening.” Rita believed that. Not because it was noble. Because it was child logic sharpened by neglect. She dried her arm, taped a pad over the cut, and went into Eunice’s tiny office. A desktop computer the church should have retired ten years ago hummed under a saint calendar and a jar of peppermints. Rita slid the memory card into an adapter and opened the files. The still image from the construction site came up larger this time. Not just a body. A man in county utility coveralls, face turned partly to mud. Next file: ledger sheets hidden as change orders. Next: a video clip shot from inside a truck, grainy but clear enough to catch Doran at a night dig site, Kearse’s deputy cruiser in the background, Mercer arguing with someone off-camera. Then the audio file. A woman’s voice: agitated, likely Celia. A man: Mercer, drunk or scared. Then Doran, unmistakable even tinny through bad speakers. You think because it was an accident that makes him less dead? Mercer: I’m not saying that. Doran: Then stop acting like your conscience is premium material. Another voice, smooth and bored. Kearse. We move him, we fix the permit timeline, and we all keep paying our taxes. Rita shut it off. Enough. She printed nothing. Too slow. Too fragile. Instead she transferred copies to the prepaid phone and another old church USB Eunice had in a drawer labeled RECEIPTS. Then she pocketed all of it. When she came back into the shelter room, Ava was half-curled on the cot but still awake. Eunice sat beside her reading a devotional with the expression of a woman who had long ago ceased expecting the Lord to improve people substantially. “What now?” Ava asked. Rita leaned against the cinderblock wall. “Now I go ask one of Mick’s men where to find him before he asks harder where to find you.” Ava sat up. “No.” “That wasn’t a democracy briefing.” “They’ll kill you.” “They’ve been trying the amateur version.” Ava looked furious enough to cry and too proud to do it. “You said not to trust anybody, and now you’re leaving me in a church.” “With Eunice.” “That’s still somebody.” Good, Rita thought. The child was learning. Eunice closed her devotional. “Baby, if men come through that door, they’re not taking you without making my paperwork complicated.” Ava stared at her. “Do you have a gun?” “I have opinions and a Louisville Slugger. Same effect up close.” That got a short laugh out of the girl, shocked out of her. Rita crouched in front of the cot, not softening her face because that never worked on children like this. “Listen. The only reason you’re breathing easier than your parents right now is because men like Doran still think they’re dealing with the kind of woman who waits to be told what to do. I need to keep that mistake expensive.” Ava looked at her for a long second. “Will you come back?” There it was. Not please. Not promise. Just the one question she could stand to ask. “Yes,” Rita said. That answer meant something because she said it plain. She stood, took Denny’s phone and keys from her pocket, and handed the prepaid to Eunice. “If I don’t answer in one ring, you call this number and send the files to every reporter, preacher, and bored divorcee in the county.” Eunice nodded. “I know some dangerous women.” “I know.” Outside, the storm had moved east into a steadier rain. The air felt scrubbed raw. Rita drove Denny’s phone to the edge of the industrial strip and followed his map history, because stupid men let their phones tell on them. Construction yard. Supply trailer. Thirty minutes away at the edge of Doran’s newest development, where marsh became lot line under enough money. The yard was lit by one security flood and the blue blink of a vending machine in a trailer office. A stack of PVC pipes gleamed wet as bones. Somewhere nearby a generator throbbed. Rita parked in the dark behind a dozer, checked the small knife in her boot, and limped toward the trailer with the same old cold room opening inside her. A shadow moved past the frosted window. Good. Somebody was home.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 5 is a strong piece of pulp fiction writing that largely delivers on its plan and character profiles. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter opens with a confident, specific sense of place. "Saint Bartholomew's annex hall sat two blocks from the bayou behind a chain-link fence and a row of crape myrtles that had survived more storms than most marriages" is a good line—the comparison earns its weight because it's grounded in the setting's logic (storms, endurance, Southern rot) rather than being decorative. It doesn't feel forced. Eunice Bell is immediately vivid and consistent with her profile. The T-shirt gag ("JESUS LOVES YOU, EVERYBODY ELSE THINKS YOU'RE A PROBLEM") is exactly the kind of mean, earned humor the plan called for. Her dialogue throughout—"Baby, if you brought trouble in here, at least wipe its feet," "I have opinions and a Louisville Slugger. Same effect up close"—is sharp and character-specific without tipping into quippiness. She feels like a person, not a device. The Rita-Ava dynamic is handled well. The exchange about Ava's father is the chapter's emotional core, and it works because it's delivered obliquely. "Children in rich houses always heard through vents" is a clean, specific observation that earns its place. Ava's revelation about taking the card—"I thought maybe if I had something, somebody would tell me what was happening"—is psychologically precise and consistent with her profile: the child who steals information to feel agency in a world that withholds it. Rita's investigation of the card is handled efficiently. The chapter doesn't linger on exposition; it gives us enough to understand the stakes (a covered-up death, Doran's voice on audio, Kearse's complicity) without becoming a data dump. The decision to transfer files to multiple locations rather than print them is a small but smart practical detail that feels true to Rita's character. The farewell scene between Rita and Ava is the chapter's best moment. "Will you come back? Not please. Not promise. Just the one question she could stand to ask." This is genuinely moving without being sentimental. Rita's plain "Yes" lands because the narration has established that she doesn't make promises she can't cash. The emotional restraint is earned. The ending is appropriately lean and ominous. "A shadow moved past the frosted window. Good. Somebody was home." The chapter closes on forward momentum without over-dramatizing. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is competent and consistent, but it occasionally settles for the expected beat rather than the surprising one. The structure—arrive at shelter, exposition dump about the card, emotional farewell, drive to confrontation—is clean but somewhat predictable. The plan called for Rita to "corner the surviving intruder from the house attack, now getting patched up in a trailer behind Doran's construction lot" and conduct an interrogation that reveals key information. The chapter ends before this happens, meaning the chapter's promised dramatic content (the interrogation, the message-sending, Doran's realization) is deferred. This is a significant deviation from the chapter plan, which specified that Chapter 5 would include the interrogation and the wrecking of Doran's operation. Instead, Chapter 5 ends at the trailer door. This means the chapter is essentially a transitional chapter rather than the action chapter the plan promised. Some lines are slightly over-written. "The water in the basin ran pink and then clear" is fine, but "the same old cold room opening inside her" at the end is a bit vague and abstract for a writer who otherwise deals in concrete sensation. "Childhood looked worst when it returned at the wrong time" is a decent observation but slightly on-the-nose for this voice. The Doran audio transcript is functional but the dialogue there ("You think because it was an accident that makes him less dead?") is a little on-the-nose as villain exposition. It tells us exactly what we need to know but doesn't surprise us. The chapter's pacing is slightly uneven—the shelter section is rich and well-developed, but the transition to the construction yard feels rushed, as if the chapter ran out of space for what it promised. OVERALL: This is confident, well-crafted pulp fiction with strong character work, good dialogue, and a clear sense of place and voice. Its main weakness is that it doesn't fully deliver on its own chapter plan—the promised interrogation and escalation are deferred to the next chapter. The prose is clean and mostly avoids purple excess. The characters are consistent with their profiles. The emotional beats are earned rather than manufactured. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 9 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The trailer door was unlocked. That told Rita one of two things: Denny was inside and hurting enough to forget basic fear, or the men around Mick Doran believed the world was still arranged in their favor. Either way, good. She went in sideways. A portable AC unit rattled in the corner, losing badly against the wet heat. The trailer smelled like antiseptic, diesel, and male panic. Denny sat shirtless on a vinyl bench with his broken wrist splinted in duct tape and a bag of melting ice on his face. A younger man in a tank top stood over him with a stapler kit and the expression of someone doing medicine as revenge. Neither saw her until she kicked the door shut. The younger man turned with the staple gun still in his hand. “Who the—” Rita hit him in the throat with the flashlight and caught him by the collar as he folded, using his body to ram Denny backward into the bench and wall. The ice bag burst. Meltwater and blood went everywhere. Denny made a noise like a sick dog. “Jesus Christ.” “Still no,” Rita said. The younger man clawed at her wrist. She drove her knee into his sternum and he went flat on the floor trying to remember lungs. Rita picked up the staple gun, aimed it at Denny’s cheek, and smiled without warmth. “Let’s try this without your usual team spirit.” Denny looked worse in fluorescent light: one eye sealed nearly shut, lips split, skin going waxy around the edges. Fear had thinned him. “I told you what I knew.” “You told me what got me out of your kitchen fastest.” She pressed the staple gun hard enough to dimple skin. “Where’s Doran taking the girl?” Denny blinked. “What girl?” Rita fired one staple into the vinyl seat by his thigh. He yelped and recoiled. “Fuck!” “The one from Bell Street. You tell Mick she had the card?” He hesitated. There it was. Rita didn’t raise her voice. “You get one more lie before your face starts looking decorative.” “He knows,” Denny said, words tripping now. “Okay? He knows the kid had it. Kearse said the old church was a good hold because nobody’d think to watch it twice.” For one beat the trailer got very quiet except for the AC choking. Rita felt the world tilt narrow. “When?” Denny swallowed. “Tonight.” “How long ago?” He looked at the wall clock automatically. Nine-eighteen. Rita had left Saint Bartholomew’s at eight-thirty. The younger man on the floor was trying to crawl. She stepped on his hand without looking and he stopped. “Where are they taking her?” “I don’t know, I swear—” Rita drove the staple gun into the broken wrist. Denny screamed so hard his voice gave out halfway through. He folded over his lap, breath sawing. “Where.” “The pump station!” he choked. “Old retention pump off County Nine, before the bridge. They use it for transfers because nobody goes there at night.” Rita stepped back. The inside of her chest felt airless and enormous at once. “You should’ve gone farther,” Denny whispered, crying now from pure body, not sorrow. “You should’ve taken the kid and run.” “Maybe,” Rita said. She hit the younger man once behind the ear on her way out and left them both alive because the night had changed shape. The road to County Nine was all black water and intermittent sodium light. Rita drove too fast with both windows cracked, rain smell and marsh smell coming in hard. She called the prepaid once. Eunice picked up on the first ring, breathing like she had come from violence. “They came,” Eunice said. Rita gripped the wheel until her hand ached. “Ava?” “Took her. Deputy and two others. Broke my office door and hit me with my own phone. Bastards tracked mud on the parish rugs.” “Are you hurt bad?” “Head wound. Pride worse. Go get the child.” “I am.” Eunice’s voice sharpened. “Rita.” “What.” “Don’t die stupid.” The line went dead. County Nine cut through a run of low marsh and new infrastructure no one had fully inhabited yet. The retention pump station sat off the road behind a locked chain-link gate and a concrete shed painted county beige. Beyond it, black water spread under reeds and construction runoff. The whole place smelled like algae, wet cement, and machine oil. Rita killed her headlights half a mile out and rolled the rest by dark. Two vehicles at the gate. One county Tahoe. One black F-150. Kearse and Doran, then. Good. She parked behind a mound of fill dirt and went the rest on foot through the runoff channel, water to mid-calf, flashlight off, gun heavy in the back of her jeans. The pump station generator hummed low, masking smaller sounds. Men’s voices carried in pieces. A child’s voice did not. At the edge of the concrete apron Rita saw them. Kearse stood near the open shed door, jacket off, shirtsleeves rolled. Doran leaned against the truck in a pale golf shirt gone dark at the chest with rain. Another man smoked under the eave. Inside the shed, light spilled over pipes, control panels, and Ava sitting zip-tied to a metal chair, the orange weather radio on the floor by her sneaker. Ava’s face was blotched from crying or rage. Hard to tell from there. Doran said, “The thing about children is they always tell the truth eventually.” Kearse looked toward the dark marsh. “Depends who asks.” Rita moved. The smoking man died first because he was easiest. She came up behind him through the generator noise, hooked his chin, and cut deep under the ear with the boot knife before he understood the scene had changed. He collapsed against the wall making a surprised animal sound. Kearse spun. “Hey—” Rita fired from the dark and took him high in the shoulder. He slammed into the shed doorframe, swearing. Doran dove behind the truck with far less dignity than his polo implied. Ava screamed, “Rita!” “Down!” Rita shouted. The yard exploded into noise. Kearse was reaching left-handed for his weapon. Rita advanced through the rain, firing once more. The truck’s windshield crazed. Doran shouted for somebody who was already leaking out under the eave. Kearse got his gun up and fired blind. Concrete chipped near Rita’s head. She dropped behind a bollard, breath coming hard and cold. “Rita,” Doran called from behind the truck, trying for command and landing on panic, “you are making this much worse than it needs to be.” “Too late,” she said. Ava was crying now in furious little gasps. “He took my radio.” “I see that.” Kearse moved wrong for a wounded man—disciplined enough to flank. Rita caught the motion in the pump-house window reflection and pivoted just as he came around the shed corner. They hit each other hard, close enough for guns to become bad tools. His forearm crushed her throat. Her bad knee almost buckled. “You insane bitch,” he hissed, hot breath and blood. She drove her thumb into the bullet wound in his shoulder. He made a sound she would think about later and lost the arm for half a second. Enough. Rita head-butted him in the nose and took him down on the slick concrete. The gun skidded away. He was stronger, bigger. She was lower and angrier. She got on top long enough to smash his face against the apron once, twice, then had to roll as Doran fired from under the truck. The shot punched sparks from the pump casing. Rita crawled to the shed, kicked the fallen gun inside, and slashed Ava’s zip ties with the knife. “You okay?” Ava nodded too fast. “He said my mom left town.” “She probably did.” Ava absorbed that with one terrible blink. Outside, Kearse was groaning and Doran’s truck engine turned over. Rita looked at Ava, at the wet radio on the floor, at the open road beyond the gate. “He’s running,” Ava said. “Yes.” Ava picked up the radio and held it out like a relic. “Then go.” Rita took it, set it in Ava’s lap, and went back into the rain.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of pulp fiction writing that largely delivers on its plan and maintains the voice established in the character profiles. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter opens with a sharp, economical hook: "The trailer door was unlocked. That told Rita one of two things." This is exactly the kind of lean, functional prose the plan called for—no wasted motion, immediate establishment of Rita's analytical mind. The violence in the trailer is quick, ugly, and practical, consistent with the character profile's insistence that Rita's violence be "bodily specific" rather than action-hero flourish. The dialogue is largely excellent. Lines like "Still no" (responding to Denny's "Jesus Christ"), "You get one more lie before your face starts looking decorative," and Eunice's "Don't die stupid" are sharp, earned, and character-consistent. Eunice's brief phone call is particularly well-handled—her complaint about the parish rugs amid genuine crisis is exactly the kind of specific, humanizing detail that makes characters feel real rather than functional. The chapter follows its plan closely: Rita interrogates the surviving intruder, learns Ava has been taken, rushes to the pump station, rescues Ava in a messy confrontation. The plan called for Kearse to escape or be wounded, and Doran to retreat—both happen. The plan specified Ava should have a "child-sized role," and her moment of handing Rita the radio while saying "Then go" is appropriately sized: meaningful without being implausibly heroic. The setting work is solid. The pump station—"county beige," algae smell, wet cement, machine oil—is specific and unglamorous in the right way. The fight choreography is coherent and grounded: Rita uses a reflection in the pump-house window to anticipate Kearse's flanking move, which is the kind of practical detail that makes action feel earned rather than cinematic. Ava's line "He said my mom left town" followed by "She probably did" and "Ava absorbed that with one terrible blink" is the chapter's best emotional moment—economical, devastating, and consistent with both characters. It doesn't over-explain or sentimentalize. WEAKNESSES: The chapter has some moments where the prose reaches slightly beyond what it earns. "The inside of her chest felt airless and enormous at once" is a bit strained—the metaphor is trying to convey a specific emotional state but the pairing of "airless" and "enormous" is slightly awkward rather than precise. Similarly, "the world tilt narrow" is evocative but borders on the kind of compressed metaphor that can feel like affectation. The killing of the smoking man is handled very quickly—"She came up behind him through the generator noise, hooked his chin, and cut deep under the ear with the boot knife before he understood the scene had changed." This is efficient but the plan called for the chapter to be "the story's ugliest turn" and for Rita to "snap from controlled retaliation into intimate vengeance." The chapter delivers competent action but the emotional register of Rita having "snapped" doesn't fully come through. She remains controlled throughout, which is consistent with her character but slightly undercuts the plan's promise of a darker emotional shift. The Doran characterization during the action is slightly thin—he fires from under the truck and runs, which is appropriately cowardly, but his voice ("you are making this much worse than it needs to be") while functional doesn't quite land with the specific menace his profile promises. He feels more like a plot mechanism than a character in this chapter. The chapter plan specified that "Ava sees more of Rita than either of them wanted"—this is partially delivered but could be sharper. Ava witnesses violence but the emotional weight of that witnessing is compressed into a few lines. The pacing is very strong overall. The chapter moves efficiently from interrogation to phone call to action without wasted connective tissue. The Eunice phone call is brief but does real work—establishing she's alive (injured but not dead, which is a slight deviation from the plan that said she might be "injured or killed"), giving Rita information, and providing a moment of human connection. OVERALL ASSESSMENT: This is confident, well-executed pulp fiction writing. It follows the plan faithfully, maintains character consistency, delivers on the action promised, and contains several genuinely excellent small moments. Its weaknesses are minor: occasional slightly strained metaphor, a protagonist who remains perhaps too controlled for the chapter's stated emotional register, and a villain who functions more as obstacle than character. The dialogue is largely strong. The prose is lean and purposeful. This is the work of a writer who understands the genre and has genuine craft. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Doran’s truck fishtailed through the open gate and threw muddy water in a fan behind it. Rita got Kearse’s Tahoe started on the second turn, blood slicking the steering wheel where somebody else had already bled on it. Ava was in the passenger seat with the weather radio clutched to her chest, too shocked to argue and too angry to stay quiet for long. “You’re shot,” Ava said. “Scraped.” “Your neck is bleeding.” “Also true.” The county road unspooled black ahead of them. Doran’s taillights bobbed in the rain, then vanished around a bend lined with half-installed streetlamps and mounds of shell fill. Kearse groaned somewhere in the dark behind them, still alive if not especially useful. “Shouldn’t we call someone?” Ava asked. “With what confidence?” She had no answer for that. Rita drove one-handed and checked the prepaid with the other at the next straightaway. Three missed calls from Eunice, one voicemail she didn’t listen to, and a text from an unknown number. Just an address and a time. Harbor View Estates Sales Event 10 PM He’ll go where the lights are. No name. Didn’t need one. Somebody inside Doran’s orbit had decided the night was moving badly and self-preservation beat loyalty. Rita laughed once through her nose. “What?” Ava said. “Your Mr. Doran’s got friends who can smell smoke.” Harbor View sat on a stretch of dredged waterfront where the marsh had been bullied into pretending to be a neighborhood. Doran’s pride project. Model homes on raised pads, decorative gas lamps, banners snapping in the weather: LIVE ELEVATED. The kind of development sold with renderings of kayaks no one in it would ever own. Of course he’d run there. Men like Doran fled toward witness, not away from it. They believed enough people seeing them intact made them so. Rita turned off County Nine and onto the boulevard leading to Harbor View. Even in the rain she could see event lighting ahead, white tents trembling under the weather, catering vans, a row of polished trucks and Mercedes at the curb. Music drifted thin and absurd through wet air. Somebody was still holding the party. “Stay here,” she said as they rolled dark into the service lot behind the model homes. Ava stared at her. “No.” “Yes.” “You said he doesn’t get another night.” “I also said survival is stupid and mostly following orders.” “He’ll see me.” “Exactly.” Ava’s jaw set. Eleven years old and all blade. “What do I do then?” Rita looked at her. Really looked. The child was shaking and holding still over it with professional effort. There were red marks on her wrists from the ties. Mud on her bare shins. Somebody else’s blood drying on the church sweatshirt. “Can you use that radio’s alert tone?” Ava blinked. “What?” “To make noise.” “Yes.” “Good. When you hear shouting inside, you hit the alert and you run to the sales office with this.” Rita handed her the prepaid phone. “There are files on it. Find the oldest woman in nice shoes and give it to her. Or a reporter if you see one. Anybody who likes scandal more than fear.” Ava took the phone. “That’s your whole plan?” “It’s the child-sized part.” Ava thought that over and, astonishingly, nodded. Harbor View’s main model home was lit like a shipwrecked wedding. Through the broad windows Rita saw couples in linen, county men with drinks, women balancing canapés and disapproval. A string quartet recording leaked from hidden speakers. On the far wall, a giant rendering showed smiling families on elevated porches above impossible blue water. Rita went in through the kitchen service entrance carrying a tray she lifted off a startled caterer in the hall. No one looked at her twice. That was the useful thing about a lifetime spent being infrastructure. In a rich room, a woman over forty in dark clothes carrying something was translated instantly into harmless labor. Eyes slid off her. Conversations continued around her. It would have insulted her if it weren’t so practical. She ditched the tray in a butler’s pantry and moved through the model home’s staged perfection: throw pillows no child had ever leaned on, bowls of decorative lemons, candles no one lit. Kearse’s blood was drying tacky at the edge of her sleeve. She wiped it with a monogrammed dish towel and kept walking. Doran stood in the great room near the fake gas fireplace, one hand bandaged badly with a cocktail napkin, smiling at a bank manager and a blonde woman in white. He had changed shirts. Of course he had. The man believed in costuming. His smile slipped when he saw Rita. Only a little. Enough. He excused himself with admirable smoothness and moved toward the hallway as if he’d merely remembered another obligation. Rita let him think she’d follow and took a left instead. Deputy Kearse was in the downstairs study with the door half shut, sitting in a leather chair under a painting of a sailboat, pale around the mouth and trying to tape gauze over his shoulder one-handed. His badge sat on the desk beside a tumbler of amber liquor. When he looked up and saw her, his face emptied in a way she liked. “You do not know when to stop,” he said. “Funny. I was thinking that about county payroll.” He reached for the desk drawer. Rita shot the drawer front. Wood splintered into his hand. He shouted and half rose, then sat back hard, breathing through his teeth. Out in the great room someone laughed too loudly at nothing. Kearse swallowed. “There are people here.” “I know. Must be comforting.” “You kill me in a room full of donors and board members, that doesn’t end clean.” Rita closed the door behind her with her foot. “Leon, nothing in your life has ever ended clean. You just had a badge to drag over it.” His eyes went small and hateful. “You think you’re different?” “No. I think I’m honest.” He lunged anyway, because men like him always did one last stupid thing to preserve the story they told about themselves. He came over the desk with a letter opener in his left hand, shoulder failing mid-motion. Rita sidestepped, caught the wrist, and drove him face-first into the desk edge. His teeth hit wood with a crack she felt in her own jaw. He sagged. She kept his hand, twisted, and pinned the letter opener through the soft web between thumb and finger into the desktop. He screamed. She leaned close enough for him to smell rain on her. “That deputy voice you do? Hate that.” Then she took the heavy crystal paperweight from the desk and hit him once in the temple. He dropped and stayed down. Outside the study, the music stuttered. Then, from somewhere near the foyer, a piercing electronic squall rose over the chatter—the shrill NOAA alert tone, impossible and urgent and wrong in that polished room. Ava. Conversations broke. Somebody said, “What is that?” Somebody else said, “Oh my God, is that blood?” Good girl. Rita stepped back into the hall as panic began to ripple through Harbor View in little embarrassed circles. Phones came out. Voices lifted. The oldest woman in nice shoes was already looking for a story to own. At the far end of the corridor, through the kitchen and out the open rear sliders, Rita saw Doran moving fast across the rain-dark yard toward the unfinished showcase home on the next lot—the one still all framing and concrete and promise. He glanced back once. She followed.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 7 is a strong piece of pulp fiction writing that largely delivers on its plan and maintains the voice established in the character profiles. Let me assess it carefully across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter's greatest achievement is its tonal consistency. The prose is lean and muscular without tipping into self-parody. Lines like "Men like Doran fled toward witness, not away from it. They believed enough people seeing them intact made them so" are genuinely insightful character observations that feel earned rather than decorative. The class commentary is woven into action rather than announced—Rita's invisibility as a middle-aged woman in dark clothes carrying a tray is dramatized, not explained. Rita's voice is consistent with her profile throughout. Her exchange with Ava in the car ("With what confidence?") is exactly the kind of dry, non-performative response the character profile calls for. The Kearse confrontation is well-executed: his lunge "because men like him always did one last stupid thing to preserve the story they told about themselves" is a sharp psychological observation that fits both characters. The letter opener through the web of the hand is specific and ugly in the right way—not action-hero flourish, but practical, bodily, and memorable. Ava's role is appropriately child-sized. She's given the NOAA alert task, which fits her obsession with weather systems and emergency tones perfectly. Her "That's your whole plan?" / "It's the child-sized part" exchange is one of the chapter's best moments—economical, character-revealing, and genuinely funny in the earned, sparse way the plan calls for. The Harbor View setting is well-realized. "Decorative gas lamps, banners snapping in the weather: LIVE ELEVATED" and "throw pillows no child had ever leaned on, bowls of decorative lemons" are specific class signals that do real work. The model home as a stage for violence is thematically resonant—staged perfection as backdrop for ugly truth. The anonymous tip device (the text with just an address) is handled efficiently. It doesn't overstay its welcome and moves the plot without requiring elaborate explanation. WEAKNESSES: The chapter has some moments where the prose reaches slightly beyond what it earns. "Harbor View's main model home was lit like a shipwrecked wedding" is a striking image but "shipwrecked wedding" is a slightly strained compound—it gestures at something evocative without quite landing the specific image. Similarly, "Ava's jaw set. Eleven years old and all blade" is effective but the "all blade" construction is the kind of compressed metaphor that works better in some contexts than others; here it's fine but not exceptional. The Kearse confrontation, while satisfying, moves somewhat quickly. The plan called for settling that account "brutally in a domestic/showroom space—something ugly amid staged perfection." The execution delivers on ugliness but the "something ugly amid staged perfection" irony isn't fully exploited—we're in a study with a sailboat painting, which is fine but not as richly textured as the rest of the model home. The confrontation also resolves a bit too cleanly for a chapter that's supposed to be building toward the final act. The anonymous tip is a slight convenience—the plan didn't specify this device, and while it works, it's a mild shortcut. The chapter plan mentioned Doran fleeing to Harbor View because "men like Doran fled toward witness," which Rita herself articulates, but the tip makes it feel slightly less like Rita's deduction and more like plot assistance. The string quartet detail ("a string quartet recording leaked from hidden speakers") is good, but "leaked" is a slightly odd verb choice—it implies something more sinister than a recording playing at a party. Kearse's final moments are satisfying but the line "That deputy voice you do? Hate that" is the closest the chapter comes to a quippy action beat—it's not Marvel-level, but it's slightly more self-conscious than Rita's usual register. OVERALL ASSESSMENT: This is a well-executed chapter that follows its plan closely, maintains character consistency, and delivers on the pulp promise without becoming generic. The class texture is present and functional. The violence is specific and bodily. The child's role is appropriately scaled. The prose is controlled and mostly earns its moments. It's not a masterpiece—some images strain slightly, the Kearse confrontation could be more textured, and there are minor convenience shortcuts—but it's genuinely good pulp fiction writing with a distinctive voice. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The unfinished house next door had no windows yet, just black cutouts where windows would promise safety to future buyers. Rain blew through the frame and slapped the plywood floors. Temporary work lights glared on extension cords, making the place look less haunted than interrogated. Doran had gone in through the rear opening, shoes slipping in the mud. Rita followed at a slower pace because she knew better than to chase a panicked rich man into a dark structure as if panic made him stupid. Panic just made people honest. The first floor was all studs and shadow, plumbing lines and stacked drywall. Somewhere above, a board creaked. Doran breathing hard. Good. Rita moved through the shell with one hand on a damp two-by-four and the other low on the gun. Her neck throbbed where Kearse had forearmed it. Her knee had become a separate weather system. Somewhere behind her, muffled through rain and distance, Harbor View was coming apart in tasteful voices. “Rita,” Doran called from the dark above. “Listen to me.” “No.” His laugh came back raw. “You don’t understand what happens after this.” Rita started up the temporary staircase. “I understand enough.” He was on the second floor near what would one day be a primary bedroom overlooking expensive water. For now it was open framing, a blue tarp flapping in one wall cutout, coils of electrical wire, and a drop cloth over a bathtub waiting to be installed. Doran stood by the far opening with a nail gun in one hand and his expensive shoes white with drywall dust. It might have been funny if he weren’t pointing a compressor-fed weapon at her chest. He looked worse than she’d expected. Hair plastered. Polo ruined. One side of his face muddy. The broad civic confidence had leaked out of him. Underneath was just a county boy with money and a terror of losing it. “Jesus,” he said, seeing her fully under the work light. “Look at you.” Rita kept moving until the nail gun twitched. Then she stopped. “Was that the line?” she asked. “You rehearse that?” “Don’t,” he snapped, and then breathed himself back down. “Don’t make me do this.” She looked at the nail gun. “Mick. You paid men to break into a house with a child in it.” He had the decency not to deny it. “I paid men to retrieve property. This spiraled because everyone around me got sentimental or greedy.” “Mm.” “Mercer stole from me. Kearse got sloppy. Your little church stunt turned everything theatrical.” “Did I?” “You think you’re avenging innocence here?” He barked a laugh. “That girl’s father was in it up to his neck. Her mother knew enough to run. These people are all dirty.” “Probably.” Something in her face unsettled him. Good. “You don’t get it,” he said. “This county runs on handshakes and omissions. Half the people in that room next door know exactly what I am. They still came for the wine and the river view.
