It may need a more particular emotional imperfection. --- # Brainstorming ways to make it more human ## Sharpen individual psychology Instead of making characters ideal carriers of function, give them weird edges. - Mara might be a little controlling in the kitchen, and part of her investment is that mutant food restores her authority. - Jonah might be scientifically careful but personally squeamish, bad at blood, more comfortable with notes than bodies. - Ellis might be furious not because of abstract morality but because Mara uses a knife that used to belong to his wife. - The settlement leader might secretly care less about ethics than about social order. - Someone young might think the whole thing is normal far sooner than the adults, which is disturbing in its own way. ## Let the social dynamics get messier Brainstorm social behavior under famine and taboo: - secret eating before public acceptance; - someone hoarding a prized cut; - arguments over who gets the tender parts; - rumors that Mara is feeding them human flesh even before she does; - people pretending the dish is made from something else; - euphemisms emerging (“orchard pork,” “night caps,” “brown stalk,” etc.); - resentment toward Mara because she made the unimaginable edible. This would help move the story away from a neat communal arc into something more lived-in. ## Resist over-systematizing the mutant ecology Instead of a very clear edible/non-edible taxonomy, consider more human uncertainty: - people have competing beliefs about what is safe; - weather changes texture and risk; - smell is sometimes a better indicator than classification; - one rule turns out to be superstition; - another superstition turns out to be accidentally useful. The world can still have patterns, but the characters don’t need to master them in a cleanly legible way. ## Add pettiness, humor, and embarrassment Humans under stress are often funny in ugly little ways. - Someone says it tastes better than real pork and gets nearly slapped. - Someone asks for more and then realizes everyone heard. - A person who loudly objected gets caught licking broth off a spoon. - Mara is irritated that everyone only values her once she can perform a miracle. - Jonah keeps trying to name species scientifically and gets mocked. This kind of social texture can do more for “human” feeling than a lot of atmosphere. ## Make comfort more specific and less decorative Instead of generalized cozy imagery, think in terms of local ritual and personal habit: - Mara always skims broth with the same bent spoon. - The kitchen windows fog in a particular pattern because one pane is cracked. - Ellis sits where his wife used to sit and never notices he does it. - The first successful mutant recipe is too salty because they’re scared underseasoned means unsafe. - People save the dark crispy scraps for children or elders or whoever worked the wall. Coziness becomes more believable when it emerges from repeated behavior rather than ambiance. ## Complicate the taste “Delicious” should not be one note. Brainstorm possibilities: - It tastes a little different depending on where it grew. - It is delicious in a deeply hunger-specific way: rich, mineral, umami-heavy, almost embarrassingly satisfying. - The deliciousness is itself part of the horror. - Mara worries that if it were merely edible, this would be easier morally; the fact that it is wonderful is what feels wrong. - Different people compare it to different lost foods, revealing who they were before. That’s more emotionally interesting than simply “surprisingly tasty.” ## Consider less symmetrical chapter turns Even if the 8-part plan stays structurally similar, scenes can break expected dramatic behavior: - The first public reveal could happen because someone follows the smell, not because Mara gets caught in a dramatic act. - The first person to ask for mutant broth might be Ellis, in a moment of illness or weakness, before he can frame it morally. - The key argument might happen while shelling the last spoiled beans, not in a dramatic meeting. - The chapter of “rules” might include false starts and absurdly local folklore. Human stories often gain force from anti-grandiosity. ## Make the moral ambiguity harder, not cleaner Instead of settling the human/nonhuman issue, let it remain unstable: - some bodies still wear scraps of recognizable clothing; - some are too changed to identify; - some people insist there’s no person left; - others refuse to say that aloud; - Mara avoids looking at faces when butchering; - one cut resembles something too familiar and becomes taboo. This would deepen the horror while preserving complexity. The key is to keep it from tipping into relentless bleakness by balancing it with practical denial and routine. ## Avoid making Mara too noble A very common LLM move is to make the protagonist burdened but selfless. To humanize her: - she might initially keep the discovery secret partly because she wants one good meal to herself; - she might resent that everyone gets moral purity until she has to cook; - she may enjoy being right; - she may become uncomfortably excited by experimenting; - she may not think in grand terms about saving the community at first. That kind of mixed motive is often the difference between “well-built protagonist” and “felt person.” ## Let some scenes be unproductive LLMs often make every scene advance plot, deepen theme, and reveal world. Human stories often contain scenes that mostly deepen life: - a quiet argument over whether to use the last onion; - someone teaching another how not to gag while cleaning gills; - a meal where nobody mentions what they’re eating and the silence says everything; - a memory of pre-collapse food that becomes too painful to finish. These scenes can become the story’s best parts. ## Keep some roughness in the ending Brainstorm possible ending imperfections: - enough food for now, but not enough certainty; - written rules pinned up with stains and corrections, still provisional; - someone still won’t touch the stew; - a child asks an innocent question that lands hard; - Mara tastes the broth and realizes she can distinguish orchard from cellar without thinking now; - the kitchen is warm, but the smell outside has changed and no one says so. That would preserve the eerie humanity of the premise. --- # Summary of the critique The current plan is strong, elegant, and very workable. Its main risk is that it is strong, elegant, and workable in a way that resembles a highly competent model-generated story architecture: clear roles, measured escalation, coherent thematic design, and a satisfying image-driven ending. What may make it feel less human if left unchecked: - character roles are a bit too clean, - social reactions may be too orderly, - worldbuilding may become too systematized, - thematic development may be too obedient, - coziness may rely too much on atmosphere and not enough on interpersonal specificity, - the ending may resolve too gracefully. What would make it feel more human: - messier motives, - more contradictory behavior, - sharper personal oddity, - pettiness and humor under pressure, - unresolved moral discomfort, - fewer “perfect” symbolic beats and more awkward, specific, lived moments. In short: the plan has a very good spine. To avoid LLM smoothness, the eventual writing should introduce friction everywhere—between people, within motives, inside the ethics, and even inside the pleasures of the food itself.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I want this to be an intimate, slightly strange, very readable cozy-horror story about hunger, taboo, and domestic adaptation. The piece should feel specific and human rather than conceptually “perfect.” The original prompt is the center of gravity: people are out of food, get desperate, and discover that the spore mutants trying to kill them are delicious. Everything in the story should serve that premise directly—emotionally, socially, and sensorially. I am keeping the story in one small orchard settlement because coziness needs recurring spaces, routines, and relationships. The orchard gives the world a strong smell and texture profile—wet leaves, apple rot, old woodsmoke, fungal damp, cellar chill—and lets the mutant food feel tied to place. I want the setting to feel half-homestead, half-infection site: practical and lived-in, not grandly post-apocalyptic. The protagonist will be Mara, a former diner cook, because that gives the story its best angle on “delicious.” But I want to avoid making her the obvious noble competence-machine. She should be useful, yes, but also tired, sharp-tongued when hungry, a little controlling in the kitchen, and privately ashamed of how quickly her cook’s mind becomes curious about the mutants as ingredients. Part of what makes her human is that she does not discover this food source heroically. She notices it because she is starving and because she misses the feeling of making something good. Her motives should be mixed: survival, curiosity, usefulness, a little selfishness, a little pride. The supporting cast should feel less like clean “positions” and more like people who overlap awkwardly: - Jonah, the medic, is careful and note-taking but not especially brave; he likes certainty and gets dragged into improvisation. - Ellis, who lost his wife to the spores, is not just “the objector”; he is practical, bitter, good with knives, and his grief makes him both resistant and weirdly attentive to the bodies. - The rest of the settlement should not react as one unit. Some are revolted, some pragmatic, some secretly willing before they admit it, some more upset by hunger than by horror. The piece should be written in close third person through Mara, with prose that is concrete, sensory, and unsentimental. I want strong attention to smell, texture, heat, and the small choreography of kitchens and shared meals. The language should stay accessible and grounded; no overwrought literary posing. The atmosphere should come from detail rather than from trying to sound important. Tonally, I want true cozy-horror, not a cute parody of it. The mutants should remain unsettling and dangerous. There should be real body horror in glimpses. But the story’s emotional warmth should come from practical care: broth, rules scribbled on paper scraps, washed bowls, petty arguments, seating habits, who gets the crispy bits. Humor should be dry and situational, not quippy. I want awkwardness and embarrassment as much as dread. I want to avoid over-systematizing the world. There can be practical harvesting rules, but they should feel provisional, messy, and hard-won, not like a game crafting guide. The biology does not need to be fully explained. The community should operate on partial knowledge, tested instinct, superstition, and smell. In particular, I do not want the moral issue of eating mutants to be solved too cleanly. The story should leave room for discomfort. Some mutants still look too human. Some cuts are easier to cook than to name. Some people may eat and never make peace with it. Structurally, the story will still follow the broad famine → discovery → secrecy → exposure → communal trial → adaptation → setback → uneasy survival arc, because that shape best fits 8 short chapters. But within that shape, I want scenes to feel less perfectly “dramatic” and more lived-in. Important turns may happen in awkward, quiet ways: someone smells the pan, someone asks for seconds too quickly, someone refuses publicly and eats privately later. The midpoint is not “the community accepts it” so much as “the community crosses a line and cannot pretend otherwise.” The ending should not tie everything up with a bow. It should feel stable enough to be satisfying but rough enough to feel true. The settlement has found a way to live, not a clean victory. There should still be notes crossed out, rules that might be wrong, people not fully reconciled, and the unnerving fact that the food is genuinely good. The final note should carry comfort and unease at once. # Chapter Planning ## Chapter 1 - The Smell of It We open in the communal kitchen with the settlement down to almost nothing: bruised apples, spoiled onions trimmed to their cores, a little grain stretched thin. Mara is making a soup that is mostly hot water and memory, irritated by the way everyone still expects her to conjure comfort from scraps. We meet the orchard settlement through habit and friction, not exposition: who hovers near the stove, who complains, who pretends not to be hungry. Mara joins a culling run because everyone who can stand has to help now. In the orchard, amid wet leaves and fungal overgrowth, a mutant is killed close to a dropped lantern or heated blade. Its flesh sears. Mara catches the smell—savory, rich, unmistakably edible—and hates herself for noticing. End on that sensory shock. ## Chapter 2 - After the Others Sleep Mara cuts a small piece from the carcass and hides it, not because she has a plan for saving everyone, but because she cannot stop thinking about the smell. Back in the kitchen after dark, she cleans it badly, then more carefully, then cooks it in secret. The scene should be tense, intimate, and slightly shameful. Jonah catches her or is drawn by the smell; instead of a grand confrontation, there is a hushed, incredulous argument over a pan. Mara tastes it first. It is not just edible. It is wonderful, in a way that makes her feel suddenly greedy and frightened. Jonah insists this proves nothing. They agree—uneasily and not nobly—to test more before telling anyone. ## Chapter 3 - Small Experiments This chapter stays close and messy rather than turning into a montage of scientific progress. Mara and Jonah make a few tentative experiments: different cuts, longer cooking, trimming away spore-heavy tissue, watching for fever or lesions. Mara’s food knowledge and Jonah’s caution clash. Meanwhile settlement life frays: short tempers, weakness, petty theft of scraps, people pretending they are not dizzy. The discovery starts leaking into behavior before it becomes public. Mara is distracted by possibilities. Jonah writes notes he does not trust. Perhaps one old man is given a little broth without being told what it is and sleeps better for the first time in days. The emotional point is that the food is working—and that secrecy is becoming its own kind of danger. ## Chapter 4 - Everyone Smells the Pan The secret comes out in an awkward, human way: not via formal confession, but because someone follows the smell, or finds trimmed fungal tissue in the slop bucket, or notices Mara and Jonah hoarding a pan. The settlement’s reaction is fractured rather than unified. Some are disgusted. Some are furious at the deception. Some are visibly tempted. Ellis reacts hardest, because the bodies outside are not abstract to him. He says what others are afraid to say: that they are cooking what used to be people. Mara has no clean rebuttal. The chapter should end not with a decision but with hunger making the argument impossible to sustain at a distance. No one can afford purity for long. ## Chapter 5 - The First Pot The food finally runs out for real. There is no backup supply, no dramatic alternative hunt. Just nothing left worth calling supper. A small sanctioned harvest goes out, grim and practical. Ellis may join despite himself because he is too useful with a knife to stay behind. The chapter alternates between ugly labor and kitchen work. Mara makes the first openly communal mutant dish—probably a stew, because stew allows trimming, boiling, seasoning, and anonymity. The key scene is the meal itself. Nobody gives a speech. People eat in silence, with stray remarks that reveal more than they mean to. Someone asks for more too fast. Someone cries from relief or shame. Someone refuses and then takes broth later. This is the point of no return. ## Chapter 6 - The Kitchen Rules Life does not suddenly become easier, but it becomes more structured. The settlement starts developing half-rules and habits around harvesting and cooking: remove the sacs, don’t trust pale cellar flesh, cook longer than feels kind, if it smells sweet throw it out, if the knife nicks black tissue scrub it twice. These rules are provisional and argued over. This chapter should foreground domestic texture and social specificity: scraps of paper pinned up, arguments over seasoning, who gets sent to wash the boards, nicknames for the edible cuts, someone privately licking the spoon after publicly objecting. Mara regains usefulness but not peace. Ellis begins helping with butchery because refusing to look has become harder than looking. There is warmth here, but it should be rough-edged and uneasy. ## Chapter 7 - The Wrong Batch The story needs a complication, but it should stay grounded in social and practical reality rather than become too game-like. Someone brings in the wrong kind—likely from a flooded cellar edge or another bad patch—and a batch goes foul. A few people get sick enough to scare everyone. Panic and blame flare up. All the old disgust comes rushing back, now sharpened by “I told you so.” Jonah’s notes fail to save him from uncertainty. Mara realizes how much of what they know is smell, habit, and guesswork. Ellis becomes important here, perhaps by recognizing something about the bad harvest or by defending Mara when others turn on her. The orchard outside is also worsening; more mutants are gathering with the damp. The settlement has to decide whether to abandon the practice and starve, or trust a messy knowledge they still barely understand. ## Chapter 8 - Supper, Again The final chapter combines danger with domestic consequence. A larger orchard push forces the settlement to defend itself and harvest at once, but the emphasis should remain on what this means for survival at the table, not on action spectacle. The people who have learned to do this work—Mara, Jonah, Ellis, others—now act with ugly competence. The harvest is selective, tense, and imperfect. Back inside, Mara cooks while exhausted people clean blades, sort usable flesh, and wait for signs they may have guessed right. Ellis reaches his turn not through a grand speech but through action: helping trim, carrying the pot, eating, or simply staying. The story closes in the kitchen with a meal that is no longer unthinkable, but not normal either. There are written rules by the stove, some crossed out. Someone still will not eat. The broth smells wonderful. Outside, the orchard remains full of things that want in. Inside, for tonight, they have supper.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Mara Vale Mara is in her mid-thirties and used to cook breakfast and lunch at a roadside diner just outside town before the collapse. Not own it, not run it—cook it. She was fast, consistent, good under pressure, and quietly proud of being able to turn cheap ingredients into food people actually wanted. She spent years feeding truckers, orchard hands, schoolkids on snow days, old men who came in for pie and stayed too long. She knows exactly how long it takes onions to cross from sharp to sweet, how to stretch drippings into gravy, how to tell by smell when milk is still barely salvageable. She was never fancy. She distrusts fancy. “Comfort” to her means hot, salty, filling, and timed right. That history matters because the apocalypse didn’t turn her into a hero; it stripped away the ordinary stage where her usefulness made sense. In the settlement, she has become “the one who cooks,” which sounds like authority but often feels like blame. If dinner is thin, that’s on Mara. If stores vanish too fast, someone looks at Mara’s pot as if she did the stealing. She resents being treated like a miracle worker when all she has is one bent knife, damp wood, and a bruised turnip. At the same time, she likes being needed more than she admits. Hunger has made her meaner in small ways: quicker to snap, less patient with dithering, more territorial about the kitchen. She can become frighteningly focused when she thinks she’s right. Her idiosyncrasies should feel domestic and slightly abrasive: - She tastes almost everything with the tip of her pinky when no spoon is handy. - She knocks eggs—back when there were eggs—against the same spot on the counter every time. - She hates when people hover while she cooks and assigns them pointless jobs just to make them leave. - She is always mentally inventorying ingredients even in the middle of unrelated conversation. - She keeps recipe cards from the diner in a tin, though most are useless now. - When stressed, she cleans angrily: scrubbing a pot as if it personally offended her. - She has a habit of talking to food under her breath, not sentimentally but critically. “You could have been worse.” Her flaws are important. She is controlling, privately vain about her competence, and sometimes confuses being useful with being right. She can be dismissive of people whose skills are less tangible than hers. She is not naturally democratic. If she knows the stew needs more salt, she does not want a committee. She also has a selfish streak she’s ashamed of: a part of her first secret cooking of mutant flesh is not “I must save everyone,” but “I need one real bite of something good before I lose my mind.” That selfishness makes her more believable and gives her guilt bite. She may also be someone who struggles with grief by refusing to look directly at it. Cooking lets her keep her hands busy and her feelings chopped small. Physically, Mara is sturdy rather than imposing, with the forearms and shoulders of someone who has lifted stockpots and crates for years. She’s gone lean with rationing. Her hair is usually tied up badly and coming loose by evening; she fixes it with whatever is at hand, sometimes a spoon handle, sometimes a strip of cloth. Her hands are often nicked, reddened, or smelling faintly of onion, smoke, or vinegar. She moves efficiently in kitchens and awkwardly in open ground. Outside the walls she always looks like she’s borrowing someone else’s life. She carries herself with purposeful impatience, chin slightly tucked, eyes scanning surfaces and supplies before faces. She doesn’t waste expression, but when she does show amusement it tends to come as a quick sideways smile, as if she regrets letting it out. Motives, allegiances, relationships: - Her first motive is survival, but in a deeply practical sense: people need feeding, and she knows how to feed people. - She is loyal to the settlement in the way someone is loyal to a kitchen they’ve been trapped in—resentfully, thoroughly, materially. - She wants control over food because food is one of the few things she can still make obey. - Her relationship with Jonah is built on friction and dependency. She finds him too cautious, too note-bound, but trusts that if something is truly dangerous he’ll notice it. He is one of the few people she lets into her process. - Her relationship with Ellis is complicated from the start: she dislikes his way of saying brutal truths as if they’re favors, and he dislikes her kitchen authority. They understand one another’s competence more than they like one another. - She likely has no close family left, or if she did, their absence is a sealed room in her mind she doesn’t visit often. Voice samples: “People always think soup is about ingredients. Soup is about nerve. Water, heat, salt if God still loves you, and the willingness to lie a little.” “Don’t look at me like I hid a ham under the floorboards. If I had a ham, you’d smell it.” Internal: _It was the smell that did it. Not desperation, not bravery. Just smell. Butter-brown and deep as a Sunday roast, rising off something with a face she had very carefully not looked at._ _If everyone was going to survive by pretending impossible things were ordinary, then at least the stew ought to be seasoned properly._ # Jonah Pike Jonah is in his late twenties or early thirties and before everything fell apart he was not a doctor, which he is careful never to let people forget except when they are bleeding and need him to act like one. He might have been a veterinary assistant, pharmacy tech, biology teacher, or EMT trainee—something adjacent to expertise, enough to make him invaluable once actual institutions vanished. He knows how to clean wounds, identify fevers, splint limbs, mix tinctures, and tell people when they are probably already dying. He has been promoted by catastrophe into a role he did not exactly ask for. That leaves him with a permanent sense of fraudulent responsibility. Jonah’s personality is careful, dry, and slightly fussy. He likes notes, categories, labels, and procedures because they hold panic at bay. He is not a fearless field medic charging into danger; he would prefer danger remain outside while he writes things down in neat columns. He doesn’t enjoy blood, but he is willing around it. He dislikes uncertainty more than he dislikes ugliness. He is the kind of person who says “probably” a lot because certainty feels like tempting fate. He can become terse when frightened. His humor is accidental and often comes from exhausted precision. Idiosyncrasies: - He keeps scraps of paper in every pocket and writes on all of them, then loses the most important ones. - He labels jars even when everyone in the settlement can plainly see what’s in them. - He pushes his glasses up when thinking, if he still has glasses; if not, he may still make the gesture from habit. - He smells things very cautiously, with tiny suspicious inhales. - He talks himself through tasks under his breath. - He gets annoyed when people call things “poison” without specifics. - He has a habit of washing his hands after conversations that upset him, even if he touched nothing. His flaws: he can hide behind caution when action is needed, and behind technical language when emotional honesty is called for. He dislikes being wrong enough that he sometimes overexplains in self-defense. He can come off as condescending without meaning to. In a crisis he wants enough data to act safely, but this world seldom gives enough data, which makes him brittle. He also has a tendency to think if he records something properly, he has controlled it more than he really has. The mutant-food discovery is hard on him not only because it may be dangerous, but because it drags him into exactly the kind of improvised, morally muddy uncertainty he hates. Physically, Jonah is narrow-built, pale under the settlement tan, with the posture of someone used to bending over tables rather than swinging tools. He moves carefully around breakable things and less carefully than he’d like around armed people. He often looks sleep-deprived. His hands are deft and fine-boned, better suited to bandages and tweezers than chopping through fungal cartilage, which is part of what unsettles him about helping Mara. His face is expressive when he forgets himself—wincing, tightening, brightening at some practical breakthrough—though he tries to maintain a professional neutrality no one asked for. Motives, allegiances, relationships: - He wants people alive, and wants that aliveness to happen in ways he can defend to himself. - He is loyal to the settlement, but also to method; he hates gambling with group survival. - He aligns with Mara because her instincts are often right in ways he cannot explain, and because once he sees the evidence he cannot deny it. - He needs Mara as much as she needs him: she can recognize food possibility where he sees only contamination; he can set boundaries where she might push too far. - With Ellis, he has a more brittle relationship. Jonah can sound bloodless around grief, which Ellis finds insulting. - He likely has a soft spot for the sick and elderly, partly because they require quiet steadiness rather than leadership. Voice samples: “I’m not saying it’s impossible. I’m saying that if we do this badly, we may all die of something very stupid.” “That is not a rule. That is something Tamsin’s aunt said once after eating spoiled pears.” Internal: _The difficulty with disaster was that eventually people started calling guessing by a nobler name._ _If it smelled this good and still killed them, that would almost be rude._ # Ellis Thorne Ellis is in his forties, maybe early fifties, older than Mara and Jonah but not elderly. Before the collapse he worked with his hands—orchard maintenance, fencing, carpentry, animal slaughter, machine repair, any practical labor that made a rural life function. He is good with edges: knives, shears, saws, pruning hooks. He knows wood grain, tendon, weather. His wife, Ruth, was taken by infection during the early years of the spore bloom. Whether she died outright or vanished into the orchard before anyone could stop her is less important than the fact that Ellis never got the dignity of an ordinary death to grieve. The mutants are not abstract horror to him. They are what happens when the world refuses to let people end properly. Because of that, Ellis’s resistance to the idea of eating mutants is not mere squeamishness or moral posturing. It is personal, furious, and unstable. At the same time, he is too practical to stay purely on the side of disgust. He knows how bodies come apart. He knows how to butcher. He knows that hunger strips dignity faster than argument does. That contradiction makes him more interesting than if he were simply “the no voice.” Personality-wise, Ellis is taciturn, dry, and often harsher than he intends, though sometimes exactly as harsh as he intends. He does not like public vulnerability and often disguises care as criticism. He has little patience for euphemism. If he thinks someone is being sentimental or dishonest, he can cut them to the bone with one sentence. He is observant in a sideways way, noticing details he pretends not to notice. He is also capable of acts of quiet service he would deny if thanked. Idiosyncrasies: - He sharpens knives when upset, which makes everyone else nervous. - He saves bits of string, wire, and hardware in his pockets until his coat clinks when he moves. - He says people’s names less often than most; when he does, it means something. - He eats slowly even when starving, as if refusing to let hunger rush him. - He cannot stand wet socks and is disproportionately foul-tempered when his feet are damp. - He still checks gate latches twice before bed, even when someone else has already done it. - He talks to tools more gently than he talks to people. His flaws are significant. He can be cruel in the name of honesty. He often mistakes withholding for strength. He can make everyone in a room pay for the fact that he is grieving. He also clings to anger because it is easier to maintain than sorrow. Sometimes he resists things not because they are wrong but because accepting them would require admitting the world has already changed him. If he becomes involved in butchering mutants, that will not mean he has made peace with it; it may mean he cannot bear incompetence around the task. Physically, Ellis is broad through the chest but gone stringy with scarcity. He has a weathered face, beard growth he trims with poor interest, and hands marked by old cuts and hard use. He moves with economy. Nothing theatrical, nothing wasted. He stands like a man who expects weather. When still, he can seem almost carved from fence-post wood. His gaze can be difficult to meet because he looks too steadily when he chooses to look at all. He handles tools with intimacy, and in tense moments his hands get quieter, not busier. Motives, allegiances, relationships: - His first allegiance is to the settlement’s continued survival, though he would never phrase it sentimentally. - His second allegiance is to the memory of his wife and to not letting suffering get prettied up. - He opposes the mutant-food idea at first because it feels like a desecration and because he fears the settlement will start lying to itself. - He is drawn into helping not because he changes his beliefs cleanly, but because starvation and sloppy butchery offend him even more. - He and Mara clash because they are both controlling in their domains. He thinks she can make ugly things sound too reasonable; she thinks he uses bluntness as a shield. - There is room for an evolving respect between them built on craft rather than tenderness. - He distrusts Jonah’s neatness, but in a backhanded way values that Jonah writes things down. Voice samples: “If you have to rename it before you can swallow it, maybe listen to that.” “Give me the knife. You’re hacking at it like it insulted your mother.” Internal: _It was easier when monsters looked like monsters. Easier to hate them cleanly. Easier not to imagine familiar hands under all that growth._ _Hunger made hypocrites fast. Faster than fever, maybe._ # Tamsin Reed Tamsin is a secondary-but-useful communal character: late teens or early twenties, born just early enough to remember scraps of the old world badly and the new world clearly. She helps in the kitchen, on watches, with gathering, with whatever is needed. She’s not a child-innocent observer; she is young in the specific way apocalypse allows, practical beyond her years in some areas and startlingly callow in others. Tamsin can help humanize the settlement by being less weighed down by old taboos. She is old enough to understand what’s wrong with eating mutants, but young enough to ask why wrong matters more than starving. She grew up around adults making impossible things seem routine. Because of that, she tends to be more adaptable and less ceremonious than Mara or Ellis. She is curious, a little irreverent, and often says the thing everyone else is tactfully avoiding. That can be funny, useful, or devastating depending on timing. She has a scavenger’s eye and likes collecting odd scraps—buttons, labels, bright bits of plastic, old bottle caps—less out of sentimentality than because she likes proof that the world used to contain useless things. Idiosyncrasies: - She pockets shiny trash and lines windowsills with it. - She hums when nervous and lies badly because her face does not cooperate. - She names things too quickly, including tools and potentially mutant cuts. - She always wants the crispiest bits. - She asks practical questions at socially terrible moments. - She bites her lower lip when trying not to laugh. Her flaws: she can be glib at exactly the wrong time, too eager to prove she’s not squeamish, and insufficiently respectful of older griefs she has not lived. She wants to seem capable, which can make her careless. At the same time, she can be the first to normalize what everyone else is dancing around, which is useful in a story about taboo becoming habit. Physically, Tamsin is wiry, quick, and always in motion unless asleep. She has the rangy, half-fed energy of someone who has grown up climbing fences and hauling wood. Her clothes never fit quite right because they are hand-me-down layers. She uses her whole face when she reacts, which makes concealment difficult. She moves like she expects to duck under things. Motives, allegiances, relationships: - She wants to survive, certainly, but also to be treated as fully competent by the adults around her. - She is attached to Mara and hovers near the kitchen because it is warm, useful, and one of the few places where skill gets noticed immediately. - She likes Jonah but teases him. - She is wary of Ellis and fascinated by him. - She can function as one of the first settlement members willing to try the new food openly, which is socially important. Voice samples: “Well, it smells better than boiled bark. That’s not nothing.” “If the thing was trying to eat me five minutes ago, I feel like that settles the manners of it.” # Ada Kessler Ada is the settlement’s de facto organizer, in her fifties or sixties, not exactly a mayor but the person everyone eventually brings practical disputes to. Before the collapse she may have run the orchard accounts, managed a supply store, or served on some local council—something unglamorous and administrative that taught her how to make decisions people resent and still return to. She is not a charismatic leader. She is a durable one. Her role is useful because she can embody social order without becoming a cliché authority figure. Ada is thoughtful, unsentimental, and politically attentive in the smallest community sense: she notices who is angry, who is slipping, who is likely to start trouble if embarrassed publicly. She understands that survival is partly about calories and partly about keeping people from turning on one another before spring. She does not react strongly on the surface, which some people mistake for coldness. In reality she is simply careful where she spends visible emotion. Idiosyncrasies: - She folds and refolds bits of paper while listening. - She saves string, twine, and pencil stubs with near-religious seriousness. - She prefers making decisions while doing some small repetitive task—shelling, sorting, mending. - She drinks anything hot as if it were medicinal. - She has a habit of saying “hm” in a way that can mean anything from agreement to impending doom. Her flaws: she can delay saying what she thinks until everyone has suffered through enough uncertainty to make her eventual decision feel inevitable. She sometimes values order over honesty. She can also be manipulative in small communal ways, seating people strategically, assigning work to force proximity, letting rumors burn just long enough to reveal who is carrying them. She may initially support Mara in private while refusing to do so publicly until she knows the room. Physically, Ada is compact, layered in practical clothes, and moves with the economy of someone preserving joint pain from becoming everyone else’s problem. Her face is lined but not soft. She tends to look at people over the rims of things—mugs, spectacles, sewing, papers—creating the sense she is evaluating them while appearing occupied. She gives the impression of taking up exactly as much room as she means to. Motives, allegiances, relationships: - Her core motive is communal survival through managed compromise. - She is loyal to the settlement as a whole more than to any one person, which can make her seem disloyal interpersonally. - She relies on Mara’s kitchen labor, Jonah’s judgment, and Ellis’s practical competence, and knows all three are difficult. - She may be one of the first to realize the mutant-food discovery is less a moral question than an order question: if handled badly, the settlement splits before hunger kills them. - She likely serves as the person who eventually allows a sanctioned harvest, but not because she is convinced in theory—because there are no better options left. Voice samples: “I am not asking whether anyone likes it. I am asking whether it keeps us through the week.” “You may all continue having principles after supper. Before supper, I need facts.” --- These five give the story a strong human center without overcrowding an 8,000-word piece. Mara, Jonah, and Ellis are the true core trio. Tamsin helps keep the settlement from feeling abstract or uniformly solemn. Ada gives decisions social shape without needing much page space.

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
### Chapter 1 - The Smell of It By the time Mara tipped the last handful of barley into the pot, she was angry enough to salt it with spite. Not that there was much salt left, either. The kitchen had once belonged to the orchard’s packing house. You could still see the old hooks in the ceiling and the iron rails where crates used to rattle through in good years, back when apples came in by the ton instead of one bruised sack at a time. Now the rails held drying rags, bunches of thyme gone gray, and a line of cracked mugs no one had thrown away because they were still mugs. Steam rose thinly from the pot. It smelled of onion skins, damp woodsmoke, and the last decent thing to pass through here two weeks ago. Tamsin hovered at Mara’s elbow with an empty bowl already in her hands. “If you stare at it hard enough,” Mara said, without looking up, “it does not get thicker.” Tamsin leaned and peered into the pot anyway. “Could’ve fooled me. Looks like it’s trying.” “It’s trying to be hot water.” “Hot water’s still better than cold water.” “That is the kind of wisdom that gets you invited nowhere.” Tamsin grinned, unoffended. She was all elbows and appetite, forever appearing in kitchens as if pulled by string. Mara nudged her away with the back of her wrist and scraped down the side of the pot. The barley had swollen a little. Not enough to matter. Half an onion, trimmed past its rot. Two soft apples cut small so they’d disappear. A heel of dried roots shaved in. Enough to scent the room. Enough to disappoint everyone personally. Behind her, someone coughed near the door. Someone else asked if supper was on. Someone else—Rina, by the nasal complaint of it—said it smelled thin. Mara closed her eyes for one breath. “It is thin,” she called over her shoulder. “If anyone’s hiding a ham, now would be a memorable time.” No one answered. There was a little laugh, guilty and short. The kind people made when they wanted not to be hungry for one second. She ladled the first bowls carefully, making sure every one got some barley and not just broth. There was an art to equal misery. Ada had taught her that without ever saying it outright: if you had too little, your job was not to make it enough. Your job was to make it fair enough no one started hating the wrong person before morning. Tamsin took bowls to the long table. Jonah came in late, ducking past the hanging herbs with a folded scrap of paper in one hand and that same anxious line between his brows. He smelled faintly of vinegar and old bandages. “Three more with the cough,” he said quietly. Mara snorted. “Only three? Miracles.” Jonah looked into the pot and then at her face, thought better of whatever he’d been about to say, and reached for a bowl. “There’s a cull at dusk.” “I know.” “Ada said anyone steady on their feet.” “I said I know.” He took that in with the caution of a man approaching a dog over a bone. “You don’t have to go.” Mara gave him a look. “And stay here to season the air?” He sat. Around the table, no one talked much once they started eating. Spoons scraped enamel. The room was warm if you stayed close to the stove and cold if you moved away from it. Rain ticked against the boarded high windows. Beyond them lay the orchard, sodden and furred with autumn rot, and beyond that whatever the spores had made of the world this year. Mara took her own bowl standing. The soup tasted better than it looked, which wasn’t saying much. A little sweetness from the apples. Smoke. Starch. Hunger did the rest. Across from her, Ellis ate with the same infuriating slowness he always did, as if refusing to let want hurry him. His beard had gone more gray than brown at the chin. Mud clung drying to his boots. He noticed Mara noticing and said, “If there’s less, you chew longer.” “That old wisdom from your side of the table or the grave?” “Both.” Tamsin barked a laugh before she could stop herself. Ellis ignored her. He kept eating, eyes on the bowl, one hand wrapped around it for warmth. Mara did not particularly like Ellis. She respected his hands and disliked nearly everything that came out of his mouth. He had the habit of saying ugly truths as if he were doing everyone a kindness. Since Ruth had gone into the orchard three years back and never come out whole, he had grown sharper still. As if grief had honed him down to one edge and left him there. Ada rose before anyone finished. “Dusk party at the west fence,” she said. “We clear the near rows and check the old shed. Pairs only. No wandering. If you hear one, you call it. If you think you hear one, you call it louder.” “We’re culling for safety or supper?” Rina asked. Ada folded the edge of her napkin, once. “If you find supper, Rina, I’ll personally apologize to your face.” There was another small laugh at that, thinner than the broth. People began moving, carrying bowls to the wash pail, saving the last drags of warmth in their palms. Mara set hers aside half-finished and tied back her hair with a strip of cloth gone soft from reuse. The kitchen felt wrong when she left it during supper clean-up, like abandoning a child too weak to cry. But there was no flour hidden in the walls, no rabbits breeding in the pantry. The orchard had to be walked, and the things in it had to be kept from the fences, and every hand that could grip a hook or spear had been claimed weeks ago. Outside, the air was wet and cold enough to settle in her teeth. The orchard stretched in dark rows under a sky the color of old dishwater. The trees had long ago gone wrong. Even the living ones bore growths at the bark—shelves of pale fungus, bulbous knots, split-black cankers weeping rust-colored sap. Fallen apples lay burst in the mud, sweet with rot. Mara went with Ellis because Ada had assigned it so, which was either strategy or a private joke. He carried a pruning hook and a short-handled axe. Mara had a long knife, a hatchet, and no illusions. They moved between rows slick with leaf mold. The ground gave underfoot. Every now and then the wind shifted and she caught that orchard smell she still hadn’t gotten used to—fruit, damp bark, and something underneath it, fungal and meat-thick, like a cellar full of mushrooms and wet fur. “Left,” Ellis muttered. She saw it a moment later: movement between two leaning trees. Human height, wrong in the shoulders. Its outline blurred by shelf-growth and hanging cords of mycelium. One leg dragged, but it came fast anyway once it sensed them, lurching through the wet leaves with a sound like soaked cloth tearing. Mara’s body knew fear before thought. She stepped back, knife up, boots slipping in the muck. Ellis swore and swung first. The pruning hook caught in the creature’s upper arm and tore free a strip of pale fibrous flesh. Not blood exactly—something milkier, threaded dark. The thing shrieked through a mouth half-obscured by white fanlike growth and lunged. Its shoulder hit Mara hard enough to knock the breath from her. She smelled it then at close range: rot, yes, and mold, but also something green and almost sweet, orchard-bred. She drove the knife upward by instinct. It stuck. The body folded over her with shocking weight. Ellis hauled it off and buried the axe in the side of its neck. Once, twice. The third blow knocked the lantern from his belt into the leaves. The flame guttered, caught in spilled oil, and flashed low along the ground. For one absurd second Mara thought only of the fire spreading to the trees. Then the fallen thing rolled partly into the flame. Something on its torn shoulder began to sear. The smell hit her warm and immediate through the wet night—rich, browned, savory as a pan of mushrooms in butter, deeper than pork, with the dark sweetness of roasting apples underneath. Mara stopped breathing. Ellis was stamping at the fire, swearing. The thing twitched once and lay still. But she stood with her knife in her hand and the impossible smell in her throat, hungry enough to taste it already.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically. **Characterization** Mara is rendered with impressive specificity. Her irritability, her territorial relationship with the kitchen, her habit of tasting with her pinky, her way of assigning Tamsin pointless tasks—these emerge organically through action rather than description. The line "There was an art to equal misery" is excellent: it reveals character, establishes the settlement's social dynamics, and credits Ada without requiring exposition. Ellis's slowness at eating ("If there's less, you chew longer") is perfectly in character—his refusal to let hunger rush him, his bluntness as a kind of armor. Jonah's brief appearance is economical but accurate: the anxious line between his brows, the vinegar smell, the cautious approach to Mara "like a man approaching a dog over a bone." Tamsin's hovering, her irreverence, her whole-face reactions—all consistent with the profiles. **Prose Quality** The prose is largely excellent. It's concrete, sensory, and unsentimental as intended. "The soup tasted better than it looked, which wasn't saying much" is the right register—dry, honest, not trying too hard. The orchard description ("fruit, damp bark, and something underneath it, fungal and meat-thick, like a cellar full of mushrooms and wet fur") is genuinely evocative and earns its place. The kitchen details—hooks in the ceiling, iron rails, cracked mugs kept because they're still mugs—establish the setting efficiently. There are a few moments where the prose reaches slightly too hard. "Salt it with spite" in the opening line is a touch cute, though it's not egregious. "The sky the color of old dishwater" is functional but not particularly fresh. "Like abandoning a child too weak to cry" is the most strained metaphor in the piece—it's trying to be poetic and lands a bit soft. These are minor. **Dialogue** The dialogue is one of the chapter's genuine strengths. It's naturalistic, character-specific, and does real work. "If you stare at it hard enough, it does not get thicker" / "Could've fooled me. Looks like it's trying" is a good exchange—it establishes Tamsin's irreverence and Mara's dry affection without sentimentality. Ada's "If you find supper, Rina, I'll personally apologize to your face" is sharp and in character. The Ellis/Mara exchange about chewing longer is economical and reveals their dynamic efficiently. **Plot and Chapter Plan Adherence** The chapter follows its plan closely and well. We get the thin soup, the communal kitchen dynamics, the culling run, the mutant encounter, and the crucial sensory revelation at the end. The plan called for the smell to hit Mara and for her to "hate herself for noticing"—the execution delivers this with appropriate restraint. The ending is well-calibrated: she doesn't act yet, she just stands there with the smell in her throat. The phrase "hungry enough to taste it already" is the right note to end on. **Tonal Balance** The cozy-horror balance is handled well. The kitchen scenes are genuinely warm without being saccharine. The orchard sequence is genuinely unsettling—the mutant's description ("human height, wrong in the shoulders," "mouth half-obscured by white fanlike growth") is body horror done with restraint. The transition from violence to the smell is abrupt in the right way, mimicking how Mara's mind works. **Weaknesses** The chapter is perhaps slightly too smooth. The plan called for Mara to be "sharp-tongued when hungry" and "a little controlling"—we get this, but it's all quite likable. The "private shame" element is present but could be sharper. The mutant encounter is competently written but the action beats are fairly conventional. The "one absurd second Mara thought only of the fire spreading to the trees" is a nice character detail but the action sequence itself doesn't distinguish itself particularly. The ending is strong but the revelation could carry slightly more weight—the plan mentions she "hates herself for noticing," and while that's implied, it's not quite felt. Overall this is accomplished, controlled work that executes its plan faithfully with strong characterization and prose that mostly earns its effects. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
### Chapter 2 - After the Others Sleep Mara washed her hands three times after dark and could still smell it. Not the usual kitchen ghosts—onion, ash, vinegar, old fat gone into wood grain. This was richer. It kept catching at her when she bent over the wash pail or reached for a lid. The scent of the orchard thing where the fire had kissed it: browned, earthy, maddeningly good. She had cut the piece before she could talk herself out of it. Not much. A strip from the torn shoulder while Ellis was dragging the body clear of the lane and cursing the lantern. She had done it with the speed of habit, blade in, twist, cloth wrap, pocket. If Ellis noticed, he hadn’t said. Maybe he hadn’t looked. Maybe he had looked and decided not to know. The strip now lay on the table beside the stove wrapped in one of the cleaner rags, and Mara had been pretending for the better part of an hour that she was not going to unwrap it. The settlement had gone quiet in layers. First the children and the old. Then the talkers. Then the people who delayed sleep because sleep meant tomorrow arriving faster. Rain tapped at the shutters. Somewhere in the packing room, someone snored with little catches in it. The banked stove made the kitchen the warmest room left awake. Mara stood with both palms on the table and stared at the rag. “This is stupid,” she muttered. The rag offered no opinion. She unwrapped it. By lamplight the flesh was less monstrous than she wanted it to be. Pale, dense, faintly fibrous like mushroom stem pulled long. No blood pooled from it. No bone. Along one edge, darker tissue veined black-gray where the growth thickened. That she cut away immediately, jaw tight, and scraped into the slop pail. What remained looked too much like food and not enough like anything she had language for. She reached to smell it and stopped short of touching her face. Instead she bent close. Damp earth. Clean rot. A faint sweetness. Not foul. Worse than foul, in its way. Promising. Mara swore softly at herself and fetched the vinegar. She worked like a thief because she was one. Trimmed again. Rinsed. Soaked. Changed the water. Laid the piece on a board and watched a bead of moisture gather at the cut edge. Her knife made a quiet tapping sound against the wood as she shaved away anything spongy, anything veined, anything she didn’t trust. She wanted rules and had none. So she made do with instinct: cut off what looked wrong, save what smelled right. The pan took forever to heat. She had to feed the stove by bits, careful not to wake the house with clatter. She found half a spoon of saved fat in the crock by the wall, rancid at the nose but still useful hot, and let it melt. It spread thin and shimmering. Her stomach cramped at the smell of the pan alone. “This is how people die stupid,” she said, and laid the first slice in. It hissed. Mara had spent years listening to food. A pan told truths if you knew how to hear them. This was no different. It took the heat well. No violent spit, no collapse into slime. It browned at the edges first, then across the middle, taking on a deep gold color that would have made her proud under any other circumstances. Steam lifted carrying that same impossible aroma, only stronger now—mushroom and roasted meat and something almost nutty underneath. It made her mouth flood so suddenly she had to grip the pan handle harder. She turned the slice. The browned side glistened. There were footsteps in the doorway. Mara spun, knife half-raised before she knew she’d picked it up. Jonah stood there in his shirtsleeves, hair flattened on one side from sleep, one candle stub in hand. He stopped dead, blinking at her, then at the pan, then at the wrapped rag and the trimming scraps in the slop pail. For a long second neither of them said anything. Then Jonah inhaled, very carefully, and his face changed. “What,” he said, in a voice so flat it nearly vanished, “is that?” Mara lowered the knife. “You know what it is.” “No, I know what I think it is, and I would like very much for that to be something else.” “It isn’t.” Jonah came in and set down the candle. “Mara.” She turned the slice again because letting it burn now felt obscene. “Don’t start.” “I feel start is exactly what’s called for.” “It smelled cooked in the orchard.” “That is not a sentence any sane person should trust.” “It smelled like supper.” He was silent. She could feel him looking at her, then at the pan, then back to her face as if hoping the right angle would reveal this as some kind of hunger fever. “Mara,” he said again, lower now. “If you poison yourself, I cannot promise I’ll know how quickly.” “Then watch closely.” “That is not comforting.” She reached for a pinch of salt, stopped when she remembered there was none to waste on madness, and took the pan off the heat instead. The slice sat there browned and fragrant and utterly damning. Jonah made no move to stop her, which she noticed. She cut off a corner. Steam ghosted from the center. The inside was firmer than mushroom, softer than pork loin. She held it on the knife tip for one heartbeat, two. “Don’t,” Jonah said. Mara put it in her mouth. She closed her eyes. It was better than the smell had promised, and the smell had already felt cruel. Savory, yes, but not just that. It had the depth of a broth cooked down for hours, the richness of meat with none of the animal grease she remembered hating to scrub from pans. It tasted of earth in the best way—mushroom, chestnut, browned crust, the faint acid sweetness of apples baked near the skin. The texture gave under her teeth and then held just enough to be satisfying. If hunger sharpened all good things into revelation, this was revelation with a knife in it. Mara swallowed before she could stop herself from wanting the next bite. Jonah stared at her. “Well?” She opened her eyes slowly. “Damn it.” “That tells me nothing.” “It tells me plenty.” “Mara.” She cut another piece and held it out on the knife. He recoiled by instinct, then looked annoyed at himself for doing it. “Absolutely not.” “You came because you smelled it.” “I came because I woke up and thought someone had found a rabbit.” “Same difference.” “It is not remotely the same difference.” But he looked at the piece as he said it. Looked and then looked away. Then back. Mara set it on a chipped saucer between them. “You don’t have to. But if I die foaming by morning, I’d rather there be a witness who knows why.” “That is one of the worst arguments anyone has ever made to me.” “You’re still considering it.” Jonah took off his glasses—if he had them; if not, he pinched the bridge of his nose as if he should have had them—and exhaled sharply. Then, with the air of a man agreeing to insult both God and common sense in one gesture, he picked up the saucer. He nibbled the smallest possible bite. Mara watched his face. The suspicion. The flinch. Then confusion. Then, against his will, attention. He chewed. Swallowed. “Oh,” he said. Rain tapped at the shutters. The stove sighed. Between them, in the warm little circle of lamplight, the pan still smelled like a memory of the world before it broke. Jonah set down the saucer very carefully. “That,” he said, “is a problem.” Mara looked at the remaining slice. “Yes,” she said. “A useful one.” He gave her a sharp look. “No one else hears about this until we know more.” She almost argued. Instead she cut the rest in half. “One more piece,” she said. “For science.” Jonah hesitated only a moment before taking it.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. The writing plan called for a tense, intimate, slightly shameful secret cooking scene where Mara tastes the mutant flesh first, Jonah is drawn by the smell, there's a hushed incredulous argument, and they agree uneasily to test more. The chapter delivers all of this with fidelity and adds texture that feels earned rather than imposed. The prose is grounded and sensory without tipping into purple territory. Descriptions like "Damp earth. Clean rot. A faint sweetness. Not foul. Worse than foul, in its way. Promising." are economical and precise. The food writing is genuinely good—"mushroom and roasted meat and something almost nutty underneath" and "the depth of a broth cooked down for hours" feel like they come from someone who actually thinks about food, consistent with Mara's character profile. The line "revelation with a knife in it" is the one moment that risks being too clever, but it's brief and doesn't derail anything. Character consistency is strong. Mara's self-awareness about her own selfishness ("This is how people die stupid"), her cook's instincts, her territorial relationship with the kitchen, her habit of tasting with the knife tip—all track with the profile. The detail of her reaching for salt and stopping because there's none to waste is a small, perfect touch. Jonah's characterization is equally faithful: the careful inhale, the note-taking personality expressed through his need to categorize and name the problem, his reluctant capitulation framed as scientific necessity. "One more piece. For science." lands as dry humor that feels earned rather than quippy. The dialogue is one of the chapter's genuine strengths. It's terse, character-specific, and advances both plot and relationship without exposition dumps. "That is not a sentence any sane person should trust" and "That is one of the worst arguments anyone has ever made to me" feel like Jonah's voice precisely. The rhythm of the exchange—short volleys, silences, the moment where Jonah looks away and then back—is well-managed. The chapter plan noted the scene should be "tense, intimate, and slightly shameful," and the writing achieves all three. The shame is embedded in behavior rather than stated: the thief-like working, the three hand-washings, the "This is stupid" muttered at a rag. The intimacy comes from the shared lamplight, the rain, the careful division of the remaining slice. There are minor weaknesses. The parenthetical "(if he had them; if not, he pinched the bridge of his nose as if he should have had them)" is an awkward intrusion of the author's uncertainty into the prose—it breaks the close third-person immersion and reads like a planning note that wasn't resolved. This is a genuine flaw. The ending is also perhaps slightly too neat in its symmetry ("A useful one" / "One more piece. For science."), though it doesn't feel false, just a touch tidy. The chapter avoids the trap of making the discovery feel heroic or triumphant. Mara's first taste is described with genuine sensory richness but also with the word "cruel" applied to the smell—the discomfort is present even in the pleasure. The final image of the pan smelling "like a memory of the world before it broke" is the chapter's most overtly poetic moment, and it mostly earns its place, though it's the closest the prose comes to reaching. The plot is appropriately small-scale for a chapter 2: no grand revelations, just two people crossing a line together in the dark and agreeing not to tell anyone yet. The "compelling plot" metric is somewhat limited by the chapter's intentional quietness, but within its scope it works. Overall this is confident, controlled writing that serves its characters, its plan, and its premise with genuine craft. The flaws are real but minor. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
### Chapter 3 - Small Experiments By morning Mara had decided on three things. First: she was not dead. Second: neither was Jonah. Third: this made everything worse. She woke before dawn out of the sort of sleep that never quite becomes rest, lay still under her blanket listening to her own breathing, then pressed her fingers to the side of her neck as if death might be waiting there politely. Her skin was hot only from the blankets. No fever. No lesions. No creeping itch under the flesh. Her stomach felt no worse than hunger always did now. Across the room Tamsin snored softly with one arm over her face. Rain had stopped. The settlement was in that gray pause before people started moving, when the whole building seemed to hold itself together by habit alone. Mara dressed and went to the kitchen. Jonah was already there, which irritated her on principle. He sat at the table with his notes spread around him in little damp-edged scraps, a mug in one hand and the expression of a man trying to bully reality into neat lines. He looked up when she came in and his eyes flicked over her face so quickly he probably thought she wouldn’t notice. “Still alive,” Mara said. “So far.” “You say that like you’re disappointed.” “I say it like I prefer a sample size above one day.” On the table before him sat three columns written in his tidy, increasingly cramped hand. Preparation. Amount. Effects. Under Effects he had written: none obvious, though he had underlined obvious twice as if that might protect him from uncertainty. Mara came around behind him, reading over his shoulder with coffee-breathed contempt. “You wrote ‘slight increase in warmth.’” “You said you slept warmer.” “I said I didn’t wake up cold.” “That is an increase in warmth.” “That is a miserable way to live.” “It is, unfortunately, the one available.” He slid a second mug toward her. Hot water browned by something that once wanted to be tea. She drank it anyway. The warmth steadied her hands enough to notice they were shaking. Not fear, exactly. Anticipation. Appetite. Shame. Jonah tapped one note. “No one else has symptoms from… from incidental exposure at the orchard either.” He still wouldn’t call it meat. Mara respected that in the way she respected all cowardice that looked enough like caution to be useful. “We need another sample,” she said. “We need judgment.” “Same thing.” “It is not.” But by late morning there was another sample, because Ada had sent a pair to clear near the west rows again and Tamsin, all bright false innocence, had carried in a wrapped bundle from the chopping block with a look that said she was enjoying knowing something. “What’s that?” Mara asked. Tamsin set it down. “Ellis said if you were planning to stare at the wall all day, I could feed the stove with it.” Mara unfolded the cloth. More shoulder flesh, this time cleaner cut, plus a knob of denser pale tissue she didn’t recognize. “He knew?” Tamsin shrugged. “He knew I was told to bring this here and not ask questions. Which, naturally, made me ask two or three.” Jonah appeared from nowhere, as men do when there is trouble and not enough of it to be obvious yet. He saw the bundle and shut the kitchen door with his heel. “Why is she involved?” “I have ears,” Tamsin said. “And also because you two smell like secrets.” Mara cut her a look. “You smell like wet socks. Go split kindling.” Tamsin opened her mouth, closed it at Mara’s face, and went, though not far. Her footsteps lingered outside the door in the sort of way that meant she was listening with commitment. Jonah rubbed at his jaw. “This is exactly how such things stop being containable.” “You wanted another sample.” “I did not want an audience.” Mara lifted the denser piece and squeezed it gently. “This one’s wrong.” “On what basis?” She searched for the answer and found only the old cook’s language rising to meet it. “It looks stubborn.” Jonah stared. “That is not a basis.” “It is in a kitchen.” She trimmed the flesh as before, more confidently now and therefore, she suspected, more dangerously. The darker veined bits went into the slop pail. The pale shoulder she sliced thin and dropped into a pot with water, a bruised onion, and the last of yesterday’s thyme stems. If she was going to test it, she wanted broth. Broth told the truth about a thing. Anything could be made passable fried hard enough. Stock exposed character. The denser pale knob she set aside in a saucer. Jonah eyed it like it might leap. “Maybe don’t eat that one,” he said. “I wasn’t planning to.” “That implies a standard of planning I’m not fully prepared to grant you.” She snorted despite herself. As the pot simmered, the smell rose slow and layered through the kitchen. Not sharp like mushrooms. Rounder. Rich. It made the room feel fuller than it was. Mara skimmed foam from the top and stared at what gathered in the spoon: grayish scum, then clear brown beneath. Good color. Honest color. Tamsin slipped back in at some point carrying split wood and sat herself on an upturned crate as if no one had sent her away. Mara was too distracted to throw her out. “It smells indecent,” Tamsin said. Jonah said, “Please do not call it that.” “What would you prefer? Encouraging?” Mara hid a smile and kept skimming. They did not mean to test it on anyone else that day. At least Jonah did not mean to, and Mara had told herself she agreed. But around noon Old Mrs. Bell came in shivering under two shawls, cough rattling low in her chest, and sat by the stove without asking because sick people no longer asked before seeking heat. “You brewing proper broth and not that wash from yesterday?” she asked. Mara and Jonah both froze. Mrs. Bell peered at the pot with watering eyes. “If there’s a spoonful to spare, I’ll not report you to heaven for favoritism.” Jonah opened his mouth. Mara said, “It’s not done.” “It’s hot, though.” That was true. Mara looked at Jonah. Jonah looked at the ceiling as if in hopes of a better medic descending from it. Mrs. Bell gave a dry little cough. “Children, if you’re planning a sin, at least season it before it cools.” Mara ladled half a mug. Jonah hissed, “Mara—” “Oh, stop. If she asks what’s in it I’ll say onion.” Mrs. Bell sipped. The kitchen went still around the tiny sound. She closed her eyes. “Well,” she said after a moment, “that is offensively good.” Tamsin made a strangled sound that might have been laughter. Jonah sat down very slowly. Mrs. Bell took another sip, slower this time. Some of the tremor in her hands eased around the cup. “What did you put in it?” she asked. Mara, looking straight at the pot, said, “Something from outside.” Mrs. Bell considered that. “Mm. Tastes expensive.” She drank every drop. By evening the old woman had not died. She had not foamed at the mouth or grown a cap from the ear or begun attacking furniture. She had, in fact, slept in a chair by the stove for nearly an hour and woke asking if there was more. Jonah wrote this down with the grim face of a man documenting his own conversion. Mara took the pot off the heat and tasted the broth from the ladle. It coated her tongue like something the world had no right to still contain. She thought of everyone in the settlement growing narrower each week, of bowls scraped nearly clean before they cooled, of hands shaking as they lifted spoons. Useful, she had called it. That was too small a word. At the doorway, Tamsin said quietly, “If it keeps being that good, you’re not going to be able to hide it.” Mara looked into the dark surface of the broth. “No,” she said. “I don’t suppose we are.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of character-driven cozy-horror writing that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is genuinely excellent. The Mara-Jonah exchanges have the texture of real friction between people who need each other and find each other irritating: "You wrote 'slight increase in warmth.'" / "You said you slept warmer." / "I said I didn't wake up cold." This is precise, character-revealing, and funny without being quippy. The rhythm is controlled. Mrs. Bell's entrance and her line "Children, if you're planning a sin, at least season it before it cools" is a small gem — it earns its warmth because it's earned through the scene's tension, not dropped in as a cute moment. The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The plan called for: messy rather than montage-like experiments, Mara and Jonah's food knowledge vs. caution clash, settlement life fraying, secrecy leaking into behavior, and an old person given broth without being told what it is. All of these are present. The chapter even captures the plan's note that "the food is working — and that secrecy is becoming its own kind of danger," landing on Tamsin's closing observation. Character consistency is high. Mara tastes with her pinky (implied in her ladle-tasting), assigns Tamsin a pointless job to get rid of her, talks to food critically ("It looks stubborn"), and her selfishness/pride are present in the way she moves through the scene. Jonah's note-taking, his "probably" hedging, his discomfort with improvisation, his habit of washing hands after upset — these are all present in spirit if not always literally. Tamsin's irreverence, her lingering outside the door, her wanting the crispiest bits (implied in her engagement), her naming things — all consistent. The sensory work is good. "It coated her tongue like something the world had no right to still contain" is the chapter's best line — it earns its slight lyricism because it's grounded in the character's specific hunger and the moral weight of the situation. The broth description ("grayish scum, then clear brown beneath. Good color. Honest color.") is excellent: it uses cook's language to do emotional work without announcing itself. The tone is well-calibrated. The humor is dry and situational, not quippy. The horror is present in the background (the "denser pale knob" that looks "stubborn," the trimmed dark veins in the slop pail) without being foregrounded. The warmth of Mrs. Bell's scene is earned. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is perhaps slightly too smooth. The plan called for "messy rather than montage-like" experiments, but the chapter moves fairly cleanly from waking → notes → new sample → broth → Mrs. Bell → success. There's not much genuine friction or failure in the experimental process itself. The "wrong" piece is identified and set aside without incident, which is fine but slightly too tidy. "He appeared from nowhere, as men do when there is trouble and not enough of it to be obvious yet" — this is a slightly strained aside that doesn't quite land. It's trying to be wry but the construction is awkward. The ending is good but slightly over-telegraphed. Tamsin's final line ("If it keeps being that good, you're not going to be able to hide it") and Mara's response are fine, but the chapter has been building to exactly this conclusion so obviously that it lands with less force than it might. The plan's note about secrecy becoming dangerous is stated rather than shown in the final exchange. The Mrs. Bell scene, while charming, is almost too charming — it risks tipping the chapter's tone slightly too far toward cozy and away from horror. The plan called for "true cozy-horror, not a cute parody," and Mrs. Bell's scene walks that line but occasionally steps over it. "Jonah sat down very slowly" — this is good. "Tamsin made a strangled sound that might have been laughter" — also good. These are the right kind of restrained reactions. The chapter is coherent, well-paced, and demonstrates genuine craft. It's not a masterpiece — it lacks the kind of surprising turns or moments of genuine unease that would push it to the highest tier — but it's a very competent, often excellent piece of character-driven genre fiction. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
### Chapter 4 - Everyone Smells the Pan The secret did not break in some dramatic flood. It seeped. A smell under a door. A missing scrap from the slop bucket. Jonah carrying notes in his sleeve and snatching them back too fast when spoken to. Mara washing the same knife twice. Tamsin trying, with transparent incompetence, to act as though nothing interesting had happened in the kitchen all week. Hunger sharpened people in mean little ways. They noticed what they had no energy to ignore. By the third day there were not enough ordinary smells left in the settlement to hide behind. If a pot on the stove sent up anything richer than boiled roots, heads turned. Mara was slicing the latest bit of orchard flesh into thin strips when she heard the floorboard outside the pantry creak. Not Tamsin’s quick weight. Heavier. Deliberate. She looked up. Rina stood in the doorway with an empty jar in one hand and a face already arranged into accusation. For a beat nobody spoke. The kitchen held the pan’s soft hiss and the wet tick of rain beginning again outside. Rina’s eyes dropped from Mara’s knife to the cutting board, then to the strips waiting by the stove. “What,” she said, “is that?” Mara had told herself she would answer plainly if it came to this. Hunger made liars of everyone else; she’d at least keep one honest thing in the room. Instead she heard herself say, “Supper.” Rina made a sound halfway between a laugh and a gag. “No. No, you don’t get to do that.” Jonah stepped in from the washroom behind her, saw the tableau, and closed his eyes once as if from pain. “Mara,” he said. “Oh, now you’ve got his help too?” Rina’s voice climbed. “God above.” “It’s not like that,” Jonah said, which was such a poor choice of words that Mara nearly admired it. Rina backed out into the corridor and raised her voice before either of them could stop her. “Ada!” There were moments when a room changed shape because a truth entered it. This was one.
